zh-tw.title: 人類圖語錄 × 五型人格
zh-tw.description: 生產者、顯示生產者、投射者、顯示者、反映者——用人類圖五大類型戳中你的搞笑又中肯的金句，繼 MBTI、星座之後的自我分類新顯學

en.title: Human Design Quotes × The Five Types
en.description: Funny, a-little-too-accurate quotes for all five Human Design types — Generator, Manifesting Generator, Projector, Manifestor, and Reflector. The next personality-typing obsession after MBTI and the zodiac

items.sourceNote: Generator type — respond strategy, sacral response, satisfaction vs frustration signature

zh-tw.content:
生產者的人生不是去「追」，是「等回應」。

對的事會讓你身體一亮、自動想點頭；不對的事，你連伸手都懶。

越主動出擊越累，越順著回應走越有電——這不是被動，是省力。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個總覺得「我是不是不夠努力」的生產者朋友，他需要的不是更拚，是更會等。
variations: 生產者最大的誤會：以為人生要主動衝。結果越衝越空，越等回應越滿足。

en.content:
A Generator's life isn't about chasing — it's about responding.

The right thing lights you up and your body just wants to say yes. The wrong thing? You can't even lift a finger.

The harder you push, the more drained you feel. The more you follow what you respond to, the more energy you have. That's not passive — that's efficient.
en.editorNote: Send to the Generator friend who keeps thinking 'maybe I'm just not trying hard enough.' They don't need to grind harder — they need to wait better.
variations: The biggest Generator myth: that you have to go out and force life to happen. Turns out the more you chase, the emptier it feels.

items.sourceNote: Generator — sacral energy only charges for things you genuinely love; burnout from doing things out of obligation

zh-tw.content:
生產者就像一顆電池：做喜歡的事會自己充電，做不喜歡的事會漏電到天亮。

所以別問生產者「為什麼這麼累」，先問他「最近在做的是不是不想做的事」。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合配上累癱的貼圖，傳到那種「明明沒做什麼卻很累」的工作群組。
variations: 生產者沒有「忙到累」這種事，只有「做不喜歡的事累」這種事。

en.content:
A Generator is basically a battery: doing what you love charges you up, doing what you don't drains you till sunrise.

So don't ask a tired Generator 'why are you so exhausted' — ask 'is what you're doing something you actually want to do?'
en.editorNote: Pair with a collapsed-on-the-desk sticker and drop it in the 'I did nothing today but I'm wrecked' work chat.
variations: Generators don't get 'busy-tired.' They get 'doing-something-they-hate-tired.'

items.sourceNote: Manifesting Generator — multitasking, skipping steps, three-minute enthusiasm reframed as a gift

zh-tw.content:
顯示生產者同時開十個分頁、學三種才藝、追兩部劇，還能煮飯。

別人說他「三分鐘熱度」，其實他只是比別人快十倍找到「這個不適合我」。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那種興趣超多、每個都只玩一陣子的朋友，幫他正名一下。
variations: 顯示生產者不是沒定性，是用最快速度幫自己刪掉錯的選項。

en.content:
A Manifesting Generator has ten tabs open, three hobbies in progress, two shows on the go — and is also cooking dinner.

People call it a 'three-minute attention span.' Really, they just figure out 'this isn't for me' ten times faster than everyone else.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend with a hundred hobbies and zero finished ones. Give them the reframe they deserve.
variations: A Manifesting Generator isn't flaky — they're just speed-running the process of crossing wrong options off the list.

items.sourceNote: Manifesting Generator — skips steps then has to go back; impatience with linear processes

zh-tw.content:
顯示生產者做事的順序：第一步、第三步、第五步、完成、欸好像漏了第二步、回去補。

別人照流程走，他用最快的路撞牆，再用第二快的路繞回來。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合自嘲，或傳給那個總是先做再說、結果常常要回頭補的同事。
variations: 顯示生產者的人生不是直線，是先衝到終點再回來撿掉在路上的步驟。

en.content:
How a Manifesting Generator does things: step one, step three, step five, done — oh wait, I skipped step two, going back.

Everyone else follows the manual. They take the fastest route into a wall, then the second-fastest route around it.
en.editorNote: Great for self-deprecation, or for the coworker who always acts first and backfills the missing steps later.
variations: A Manifesting Generator's path isn't a straight line — it's sprinting to the finish, then doubling back for the steps they dropped along the way.

items.sourceNote: Projector — wait for the invitation strategy; classic 'not lazy, waiting to be invited' joke

zh-tw.content:
投射者：「我不是懶，我是在等邀請。」

投射者的能量設計就是不主動出擊，等被看見、被點名、被邀請的時候，才會發光。

所以別催投射者衝，他不衝是有原因的——他在等一個對的場子。
zh-tw.editorNote: 投射者朋友的最佳自我介紹，傳給那個常被說「太被動」的人，幫他翻案。
variations: 投射者的座右銘：好東西值得等，包括等別人發現你有多好。

en.content:
A Projector: 'I'm not lazy — I'm waiting for the invitation.'

Projectors aren't built to charge in. They're built to shine once they're seen, named, invited.

So stop telling a Projector to hustle. There's a reason they're not rushing — they're waiting for the right room.
en.editorNote: The perfect bio line for any Projector. Send it to the friend who keeps getting called 'too passive' and overturn the verdict.
variations: A Projector's motto: good things are worth waiting for — including waiting for people to notice how good you are.

items.sourceNote: Projector — sees through people, limited energy, needs to lie down after socializing

zh-tw.content:
投射者很會看穿別人，一眼就知道誰在硬撐、誰在裝忙。

代價是：他自己的電量只有別人的一半，社交兩小時要回血一整天。

投射者下班不是想耍廢，是真的需要躺平充電。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個聚會結束就秒消失、隔天才復活的朋友，他不是高冷，是沒電。
variations: 投射者：看得懂全世界，唯一看不懂的是自己怎麼又這麼累。

en.content:
Projectors read people like open books — one glance and they know who's faking busy and who's barely holding it together.

The catch: they run on half the battery everyone else has. Two hours of socializing, a full day to recover.

When a Projector lies down after work, they're not slacking — they genuinely need to recharge.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who vanishes the second a party ends and reappears the next afternoon. They're not aloof — they're out of battery.
variations: A Projector: can read the whole world. The one thing they can't figure out is why they're this tired again.

items.sourceNote: Projector — bitterness as the not-self theme when not recognized; reframing being overlooked

zh-tw.content:
投射者沒被看見的時候，會有一種說不出口的苦：「我明明都看到了，為什麼沒人問我？」

但投射者要記得——你的價值不是用搶來的，是被邀請出來的。

與其硬擠進不適合的場子，不如把力氣留給真正懂你的人。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合安慰那種「很有想法卻常被忽略」的投射者朋友，給他一點溫柔的提醒。
variations: 投射者的功課：不被邀請的場子，不是你的舞台，不用為它委屈。

en.content:
When a Projector goes unseen, there's a quiet bitterness: 'I saw it all — why did nobody ask me?'

But here's the thing — your worth isn't something you grab. It's something you get invited to share.

Don't force yourself into rooms that don't fit. Save your energy for the people who actually get you.
en.editorNote: Good for comforting the Projector friend who's full of insight but keeps getting overlooked. A gentle nudge, not a lecture.
variations: A Projector's lesson: a room that won't invite you isn't your stage. Don't shrink yourself to stay in it.

items.sourceNote: Manifestor — inform strategy, dislikes being controlled/questioned, anger as not-self theme

zh-tw.content:
顯示者的策略只有兩個字：告知。

不是徵求同意，是「我要去做了，先讓你知道一聲」。

顯示者最受不了的就是被問「你要幹嘛、為什麼、可不可以」——他不是不講理，他只是天生不需要許可。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個做決定從不問人、但會事後通知你的朋友，他不是白目，是顯示者。
variations: 顯示者的禮貌不是「請問可以嗎」，是「我先說一聲讓你有心理準備」。

en.content:
A Manifestor's whole strategy is one word: inform.

Not ask for permission — just 'I'm about to do this, giving you a heads-up.'

The fastest way to set off a Manifestor is to ask 'what are you doing, why, can you even?' They're not difficult — they're just wired to not need approval.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who never asks before deciding but does tell you afterward. They're not rude — they're a Manifestor.
variations: A Manifestor's version of polite isn't 'may I?' — it's 'heads-up, so you're not caught off guard.'

items.sourceNote: Manifestor — initiator energy, starts things then hands off; peace as signature

zh-tw.content:
顯示者是來「點火」的：把事情啟動、把球丟出去，然後退到一旁讓別人接手。

所以顯示者不是虎頭蛇尾，是任務分工——開頭是他的天賦，收尾交給生產者就好。

當顯示者不被擋、不被管的時候，他會出奇地平靜。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種很會開頭、很會帶風向，但細節懶得管的領導型朋友。
variations: 顯示者的角色：負責按下開始鍵，至於跑完全程，是別人的事。

en.content:
A Manifestor is here to light the fuse: kick things off, throw the ball, then step back and let others run with it.

So they're not 'all start, no finish' — it's division of labor. Beginnings are their gift; leave the finishing to a Generator.

When nobody blocks or micromanages them, a Manifestor is surprisingly at peace.
en.editorNote: Perfect for the leader-type friend who's brilliant at starting and steering but can't be bothered with the details.
variations: A Manifestor's job: press the start button. Crossing the finish line is somebody else's department.

items.sourceNote: Reflector — rarest type, wait a full lunar cycle (28 days) for big decisions

zh-tw.content:
全世界只有 1% 的反映者，重大決定要等一整個月亮週期——大概 28 天。

朋友問：「晚上要不要吃火鍋？」
反映者：「給我 28 天，我月底回覆你。」

反映者不是難搞，是真的需要時間，把每個面向都照一遍才安心。
zh-tw.editorNote: 超適合自嘲做決定很慢，或傳給那個「再讓我想想」想了一個月的朋友。
variations: 反映者選餐廳的速度，跟等一輪月圓月缺差不多。

en.content:
Reflectors are just 1% of the planet, and big decisions take them a full lunar cycle — about 28 days.

Friend: 'Hotpot tonight?'
Reflector: 'Give me 28 days. I'll get back to you end of month.'

They're not difficult — they genuinely need time to view a choice from every angle before it feels right.
en.editorNote: Perfect for joking about being a slow decider, or for the friend whose 'let me think about it' has lasted a month.
variations: A Reflector picks a restaurant at roughly the speed of one full moon to the next.

items.sourceNote: Reflector — mirror nature, reflects the health of their environment; change environment, change person

zh-tw.content:
反映者像一面鏡子，照出身邊環境的狀態：環境健康，他就閃閃發光；環境烏煙瘴氣，他第一個垮掉。

所以反映者換個環境，常常像換了個人。

別問反映者「你怎麼了」，先看看他最近待在什麼樣的地方、跟什麼樣的人。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合提醒那種「待錯地方就整個人黯淡」的朋友，環境對他真的很重要。
variations: 反映者的情緒晴雨表，量的其實是周圍的人健不健康。

en.content:
A Reflector is a mirror, showing the state of whatever surrounds them: a healthy environment and they sparkle, a toxic one and they're the first to crash.

That's why a Reflector in a new setting can feel like a whole new person.

Don't ask a Reflector 'what's wrong with you' — look at where they've been lately and who they've been around.
en.editorNote: Good for reminding the friend who dims completely in the wrong place. Environment matters more for them than for anyone.
variations: A Reflector's mood barometer isn't measuring them — it's measuring how healthy everyone around them is.

items.sourceNote: Sacral response — the body answers before the brain; uh-huh / unh-unh gut sounds

zh-tw.content:
人類圖教我最有用的一件事：別問腦袋，問身體。

腦袋會編一百個「應該要」的理由，身體只會誠實地給你一個「嗯—」或「呃……不要」。

那個瞬間湧上來的反應，比你想三天的結論還準。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合放在「又在猶豫要不要答應」的時刻，提醒自己相信第一直覺。
variations: 你的身體早就知道答案了，是你的腦袋還在開會。

en.content:
The most useful thing Human Design taught me: don't ask your head, ask your body.

Your head will invent a hundred 'I should' reasons. Your body just gives you an honest 'mmh — yes' or 'ehh… no.'

That split-second gut reaction is more accurate than three days of overthinking.
en.editorNote: Good for the moment you're stuck deciding whether to say yes. A reminder to trust the first gut hit.
variations: Your body already knew the answer. Your head is just still in a meeting about it.

items.sourceNote: Undefined centers / conditioning — amplifying others' energy in a crowd, being yourself when alone

zh-tw.content:
你那些「空白的能量中心」，就像沒裝紗窗的窗戶——別人的情緒會直接灌進來，還會被你放大。

所以在人群裡你常常分不清：這份焦慮到底是我的，還是我接收來的？

一個人獨處時那個平靜的你，才是原廠設定。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「在人多的地方特別累、回家才像自己」的高敏感朋友。
variations: 你不是情緒化，你只是把全場的情緒都收進來了。

en.content:
Your 'undefined centers' are like windows with no screens — other people's emotions blow straight in, and you amplify them.

So in a crowd you often can't tell: is this anxiety even mine, or did I just pick it up?

The calm version of you when you're alone — that's the factory setting.
en.editorNote: For the highly-sensitive friend who's wiped out in crowds and only feels like themselves once they're home.
variations: You're not 'too emotional' — you just absorbed the emotions of the entire room.

items.sourceNote: Not-self theme — each type has a tell-tale emotion signaling misalignment (frustration/bitterness/anger/disappointment)

zh-tw.content:
人類圖最貼心的設計，是每種人都內建一顆「走錯路警報器」：

生產者走錯了會「挫敗」、投射者會「苦澀」、顯示者會「憤怒」、反映者會「失望」。

所以那些情緒不是你的缺點，是身體在提醒你：欸，這條路不適合你，該轉彎了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合在情緒低潮時自我提醒，把負面情緒當成導航，而不是敵人。
variations: 你的壞情緒不是來找碴的，是來報路況的：前方此路不通。

en.content:
The kindest part of Human Design: every type comes with a built-in 'wrong-turn alarm.'

Generators feel frustration, Projectors feel bitterness, Manifestors feel anger, Reflectors feel disappointment.

Those emotions aren't your flaws — they're your body saying, hey, this path isn't for you, time to turn.
en.editorNote: Good for low moments — a reminder to treat a bad mood as navigation, not an enemy.
variations: Your bad mood isn't here to ruin your day. It's a traffic report: road ahead closed, reroute.

items.sourceNote: Human Design x MBTI x zodiac combo — Taiwan's obsession with self-classification systems

zh-tw.content:
現代人認識自己的三件套：星座看個性、MBTI 看人設、人類圖看能量。

聊天開場已經從「你哪個星座」進化成「你 I 人還是 E 人、你哪型的」。

我們不是迷信，我們只是太想搞懂自己，順便有個藉口解釋為什麼這麼累。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合丟進那種一見面就互問星座、MBTI、人類圖的朋友群，懂的人秒笑。
variations: 這年頭認識新朋友，不問星座 MBTI 人類圖，根本不知道要怎麼聊下去。

en.content:
The modern self-discovery starter pack: zodiac for your personality, MBTI for your persona, Human Design for your energy.

The opening line has evolved from 'what's your sign?' to 'are you an I or an E, and what's your type?'

We're not superstitious — we're just desperate to understand ourselves, and to have an excuse for why we're this tired.
en.editorNote: Drop it in the group chat that swaps zodiac signs, MBTI letters, and Human Design types on sight. The right people will get it instantly.
variations: These days, meeting someone new without asking their sign, MBTI, and Human Design type leaves you with nothing to talk about.

items.sourceNote: Strategy across types — waiting reframed as active, trusting timing instead of forcing

zh-tw.content:
人類圖教我最反直覺的一課：等待，不是浪費時間。

生產者等回應、投射者等邀請、反映者等月亮——每一種「等」，其實都是在等對的能量靠近你。

硬擠來的，留不住；對的事，會自己找上門。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種急著想「快點有結果」的時刻，提醒自己順流比硬幹有用。
variations: 你以為的「卡住」，有時候只是時機還沒到。該你的，跑不掉。

en.content:
The most counterintuitive lesson Human Design taught me: waiting is not wasting time.

Generators wait to respond, Projectors wait for the invitation, Reflectors wait for the moon — every kind of 'waiting' is really waiting for the right energy to come to you.

What you force won't stay. What's meant for you will find its way in.
en.editorNote: For the moment you're itching for results right now — a reminder that flowing beats forcing.
variations: What feels like being 'stuck' is sometimes just timing that hasn't arrived yet. What's yours won't get away.

items.sourceNote: Projector — recognition over hustle; the right one invitation beats a hundred cold pitches

zh-tw.content:
投射者不需要被很多人喜歡，只需要被「對的人」看見一次。

一個真心的邀請，勝過一百次自我推銷；一個懂你的人，抵得過一整個不適合你的圈子。

所以別急著到處發名片，那個會點名你的人，遲早會出現。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合鼓勵那種「很努力刷存在感卻很累」的投射者朋友，少即是多。
variations: 投射者的成功不是被全世界看見，是被那個對的人，剛好看見。

en.content:
A Projector doesn't need to be liked by many — just to be truly seen, once, by the right person.

One genuine invitation beats a hundred cold pitches. One person who gets you outweighs a whole circle that doesn't.

So stop handing out business cards everywhere. The one who'll call your name will show up in time.
en.editorNote: For encouraging the Projector friend who's exhausting themselves trying to be noticed. Less really is more.
variations: A Projector's success isn't being seen by the whole world — it's being seen, at just the right moment, by the right person.

items.sourceNote: Profile 3rd line — the martyr/experimenter; trial-and-error, bumping into things, learning by mistakes

zh-tw.content:
人類圖裡有「三爻」的人，是天生的試錯機器：別人看說明書，他直接動手撞牆，撞完才知道哪裡不能走。

所以他踩過的雷特別多，但也因為這樣，他講的經驗最真。

別笑三爻又跌倒了——他正在幫全人類測試這條路通不通。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個老是同一個坑跌兩次、卻又最有故事可講的朋友，幫他平反一下。
variations: 三爻的人生不是失敗，是田野調查：每個錯誤都是一筆數據。

en.content:
If you've got a 3rd line in your Human Design profile, you're a born trial-and-error machine: everyone else reads the manual, you just walk straight into the wall to find out where the wall is.

So you step on more landmines than most — but that's also why your advice is the realest in the room.

Don't laugh at the 3-line tripping again. They're beta-testing the path for the rest of humanity.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who falls in the same hole twice but always has the best stories. Give them their redemption arc.
variations: A 3rd line's life isn't failure — it's field research. Every mistake is just one more data point.

items.sourceNote: Manifestor — surge energy; either 200% full throttle or fully recharging, no in-between

zh-tw.content:
顯示者的能量是「爆發型」的：要嘛火力全開衝到 200%，要嘛整個人關機回血，中間沒有檔位。

所以別期待顯示者「穩穩地、每天一點點」——那不是他的設計。

他不是時好時壞，是來一波、走一波，浪來就衝，浪走就躺。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那種一忙就拚到極限、忙完就消失一週的朋友，他不是懶，是在等下一波。
variations: 顯示者只有兩種狀態：全速前進，或徹底躺平。沒有「溫溫的」這個選項。

en.content:
A Manifestor runs on surge energy: either full throttle at 200%, or fully powered down and recharging. There's no middle gear.

So don't expect a Manifestor to do 'a little bit, steadily, every day' — that's just not how they're built.

They're not inconsistent. They come in waves: ride the swell when it's here, lie flat when it's gone.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who goes all-out when busy then vanishes for a week. They're not lazy — they're waiting for the next wave.
variations: A Manifestor has two settings: full speed ahead, or completely flat on the floor. 'Mildly warm' is not on the menu.

items.sourceNote: 12 Profiles — conscious vs unconscious lines; who you think you are vs how others see you

zh-tw.content:
人類圖的「人生角色」是兩個數字組成的，像 1/3、5/1。

前面那個，是你以為的自己；後面那個，是別人眼中、你自己沒發現的你。

所以最有趣的時刻，是有人形容你，講的全是那個「後面的數字」——欸，原來我在別人眼裡長這樣。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合丟給剛開始研究人類圖、查到自己角色號碼很興奮的朋友。
variations: 你以為你是哪種人，跟別人看到的你，常常是兩個數字、兩個世界。

en.content:
Your Human Design 'profile' is two numbers, like 1/3 or 5/1.

The first one is who you think you are. The second is the version of you everyone else sees — the part you've never quite noticed yourself.

So the most interesting moment is when someone describes you and it's all that 'second number.' Huh — so that's what I look like from the outside.
en.editorNote: Good for the friend who just discovered Human Design and is thrilled to learn their profile numbers.
variations: Who you think you are and who others see are often two numbers — and two entirely different worlds.

items.sourceNote: Compatibility — any two types can work if both follow their own strategy and authority; relationship reframe

zh-tw.content:
人類圖最暖的關係觀：沒有「天生不合」的兩個人，只有「沒在做自己」的兩個人。

顯示者配反映者、投射者配生產者——任何組合都能很好，前提是兩個人都照自己的節奏走。

感情會卡，常常不是因為個性不合，是因為其中一個人活成了別人。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合傳給正在感情裡糾結「我們是不是不適合」的朋友，給他一個新角度。
variations: 合不合，不是看星座配對表，是看你們有沒有各自把自己活對。

en.content:
The warmest take Human Design has on relationships: there's no such thing as two people who are 'just incompatible' — only two people who aren't being themselves.

Manifestor with Reflector, Projector with Generator — any combo can be beautiful, as long as both people move at their own rhythm.

When a relationship gets stuck, it's often not a clash of personalities. It's that one person is living as someone they're not.
en.editorNote: For the friend agonizing over 'maybe we're just not right for each other.' Hand them a different lens.
variations: Compatibility isn't a zodiac chart. It's whether you've each figured out how to live as your actual self.

items.sourceNote: Emotional authority — wait through the emotional wave (highs and lows) before deciding; no truth in the now

zh-tw.content:
情緒型權威的人，當下沒有真相。

你早上覺得「絕對要」，晚上又覺得「算了吧」——不是你善變，是你的情緒在跑一整條波，高點低點都要走完，答案才會浮出來。

所以重大決定別當場拍板，睡一覺、等一波，明天再說。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合傳給那種「衝動下單、隔天後悔」的情緒型朋友，提醒他凡事先等過一個晚上。
variations: 情緒型的人最該學的一句話：「這件事我明天再回你。」

en.content:
If your authority is emotional, there's no truth in the now.

This morning it's a hard yes, by tonight it's a meh — you're not flaky, your emotions are riding a whole wave, and the answer only surfaces once the high and the low have both passed.

So don't lock in big decisions on the spot. Sleep on it, ride the wave out, talk tomorrow.
en.editorNote: Send to the emotional-authority friend who impulse-buys at midnight and regrets it by morning. The reminder: always sleep on it.
variations: The single best line for an emotional type to learn: 'Let me get back to you on that tomorrow.'

items.sourceNote: Deconditioning — takes about 7 years (cellular renewal); unlearning who you were told to be

zh-tw.content:
人類圖說，「去制約」要花大約七年——剛好是身體所有細胞換過一輪的時間。

意思是，要把那個「別人期待你成為的樣子」慢慢脫掉，回到原廠的你，本來就急不得。

所以別怪自己改變得太慢，你不是在進度落後，你是在用整個身體重新長成自己。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合安慰那種「我怎麼還是老樣子」而焦慮的朋友，提醒他改變本來就需要時間。
variations: 變回自己沒有捷徑，七年起跳——你不是慢，是正在重新長一遍。

en.content:
Human Design says deconditioning takes about seven years — roughly how long it takes every cell in your body to renew.

Meaning: peeling off 'the version of you everyone expected' and getting back to the factory you was never going to be quick.

So stop blaming yourself for changing slowly. You're not behind schedule — you're regrowing into yourself with your entire body.
en.editorNote: For the friend anxious that 'I'm still the same old me.' A reminder that real change was always meant to take time.
variations: There's no shortcut back to yourself — figure seven years, minimum. You're not slow, you're regrowing from scratch.

items.sourceNote: Inner authority — body-based intelligence that knows before the mind; trust the body over the head

zh-tw.content:
你的「內在權威」是身體的智慧，它在腦袋開口之前就已經知道答案了。

問題是我們從小被訓練成「凡事用想的」，把身體那個誠實的訊號當雜訊蓋掉。

做決定卡關時，試試把問題唸出來，感覺一下身體是放鬆還是緊縮——它比你的小劇場可靠多了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「列了三頁優缺點還是選不出來」的朋友，提醒他答案不在表格裡。
variations: 你的腦袋負責找理由，你的身體負責知道答案——別把這兩件事搞反。

en.content:
Your inner authority is a body-based intelligence — it knows the answer before your mind even opens its mouth.

The trouble is we're trained from childhood to 'think it through,' so we treat that honest signal from the body as noise and mute it.

Next time a decision stalls, say the question out loud and notice whether your body loosens or tightens. It's far more reliable than the soap opera in your head.
en.editorNote: For the friend who made a three-page pros-and-cons list and still can't choose. The answer was never in the spreadsheet.
variations: Your head's job is to find reasons. Your body's job is to already know the answer. Stop getting those two backwards.

items.sourceNote: Manifestor aura — outward and repelling; people unconsciously give them space; explains the 'intimidating' read

zh-tw.content:
顯示者的氣場是「往外推」的，所以常常莫名其妙被說「看起來很兇」、「不好親近」。

其實他沒在生氣，只是那股氣場天生就會幫他清出一條路、留出一點距離。

別誤會顯示者高冷——他只是自帶結界，不是故意把你推開。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個老被說「臉很臭、其實人很好」的朋友，幫他解釋一下那股氣場。
variations: 顯示者不是擺臭臉，是氣場自動開啟了「保持距離」模式。

en.content:
A Manifestor's aura pushes outward, which is why they keep getting read as 'intimidating' or 'hard to approach' for no obvious reason.

They're not actually angry — that aura just naturally clears a path and keeps a little distance around them.

So don't mistake a Manifestor for aloof. They come with a built-in force field; they're not pushing you away on purpose.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who's always told they have 'resting unapproachable face' but is actually lovely. Explain the aura for them.
variations: A Manifestor isn't giving you attitude — their aura just defaults to 'keep your distance' mode.

items.sourceNote: Projector energy — 3-4 hours of focused work beats 8 hours of forced output; quality over quantity of energy

zh-tw.content:
投射者的能量設計不是「撐久」，是「夠準」：專注的三四個小時，勝過硬撐的八小時。

所以投射者朝九晚五會被榨乾，但如果只在電量最滿的時段火力全開，產出反而嚇死人。

別逼投射者跟生產者比工時——他比的是命中率，不是耐力。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「工時最短、成果卻最漂亮」的投射者朋友，幫他理直氣壯地早下班。
variations: 投射者的高效不是坐好坐滿，是在對的三小時裡，把事情一槍命中。

en.content:
A Projector isn't built to last long — they're built to land precisely: three or four focused hours beat eight hours of forced output.

So a nine-to-five drains them dry, but let them go full throttle only when their battery's at its fullest, and the results are frankly scary-good.

Don't make a Projector compete with a Generator on hours logged — they're competing on accuracy, not stamina.
en.editorNote: For the Projector friend who logs the fewest hours but turns in the best work. Give them permission to clock out early, guilt-free.
variations: A Projector's productivity isn't butt-in-seat time — it's nailing the whole thing in the right three hours.

items.sourceNote: Splenic authority — the spleen speaks once, quietly, in the present moment, and never repeats itself

zh-tw.content:
脾型權威的直覺只說一次，小小聲、在當下，講完就不再重複。

所以你常常事後才懊惱：「那一瞬間我明明有感覺，怎麼沒聽？」

脾不會像情緒那樣跟你磨好幾天，它只給你一次輕輕的提醒——接到了就走，沒接到就沒了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「明明第一直覺最準、卻老是想太多才出錯」的脾型朋友，提醒他別錯過那一秒。
variations: 脾型直覺像門鈴：只響一次。沒去開門，它不會再幫你按第二下。

en.content:
If your authority is splenic, your intuition speaks just once — quietly, in the present moment — and never repeats itself.

So you keep kicking yourself afterward: 'I felt it in that instant, why didn't I listen?'

The spleen won't grind on it for days like an emotional wave. It gives you one soft nudge — catch it and go, or it's gone.
en.editorNote: For the splenic friend whose first instinct is always right but who overthinks their way into the wrong call. Don't miss the one-second window.
variations: Splenic intuition is like a doorbell: it rings once. Miss it, and it won't ring again for you.

items.sourceNote: Undefined spleen — holding onto things/people/jobs that are bad for you out of fear; learning to let go

zh-tw.content:
脾中心空白的人，最容易「明知道不好，還是抓著不放」：爛工作、消耗你的人、過期的關係，全都捨不得放手。

因為空白的脾會把「恐懼」放大，讓你誤以為「放掉會更慘」。

但其實你早就不需要它了，只是被那股莫名的怕綁住。該放的，放了你會輕很多。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合提醒那種「明知道該斷、卻一直拖」的朋友，那不是你軟弱，是空白脾在作用。
variations: 空白脾的功課：分清楚「我真的需要」跟「我只是怕失去」——這兩個常常長得很像。

en.content:
People with an undefined spleen are masters of 'I know it's bad for me but I can't let go': the draining job, the person who depletes you, the relationship past its expiry date.

An open spleen amplifies fear, tricking you into believing 'letting go will be even worse.'

But you stopped needing it a long time ago — you're just tied down by that vague dread. Let go of what's done, and you'll feel so much lighter.
en.editorNote: For the friend who knows they should cut something loose but keeps stalling. That's not weakness — that's an open spleen at work.
variations: The open-spleen lesson: tell 'I actually need this' apart from 'I'm just scared to lose it.' They look almost identical.

items.sourceNote: Manifesting Generator — Generator life force plus Manifestor speed; fastest type, gets a lot done but needs the sacral yes

zh-tw.content:
顯示生產者是「生產者的續航力 + 顯示者的速度」合體：別人還在暖身，他已經做完三件事了。

但快歸快，前提一樣是薦骨要點頭——對的事他用噴射速度衝，不對的事他連起跑線都站不上去。

所以別逼顯示生產者「快點做你不想做的事」，那是世界上最慢的組合。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個做事飛快、但只對有興趣的事飛快的朋友，幫他解釋一下那個落差。
variations: 顯示生產者：對的事用兩倍速完成，不對的事用龜速拖到地老天荒。

en.content:
A Manifesting Generator is 'Generator stamina plus Manifestor speed' fused into one: everyone else is still warming up while they've already knocked out three things.

But fast as they are, the same rule applies — the sacral has to say yes. The right thing, they hit at jet speed; the wrong thing, they can't even make it to the starting line.

So never push a Manifesting Generator to 'hurry up and do the thing they don't want to do.' That's the slowest combo on earth.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who moves at lightning speed — but only on things they care about. Explain the gap for them.
variations: A Manifesting Generator: double speed on the right thing, glacial slow-motion on the wrong one.

items.sourceNote: Not-self mind vs strategy & authority — the mind makes the worst decisions; its job is to observe, not decide

zh-tw.content:
人類圖有個很狠的真相：你的頭腦，是你身上最不該拿來「做決定」的東西。

它超會分析、超會比較、超會找理由——但它做的決定，往往最後讓你後悔。

頭腦的工作是「觀察」跟「分享」，不是「拍板」。決定，交給你的身體和直覺就好。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「想到頭都痛了還是選不出來」的朋友，提醒他頭腦不是用來下結論的。
variations: 頭腦是很棒的顧問，是很爛的老闆。讓它出意見，但別讓它做主。

en.content:
A brutal truth in Human Design: your mind is the single worst part of you to put in charge of decisions.

It's brilliant at analyzing, comparing, building airtight cases — and the choices it makes are the ones you tend to regret most.

The mind's job is to observe and to share, not to call the shots. Leave the deciding to your body and your intuition.
en.editorNote: For the friend who's thought themselves into a headache and still can't choose. A reminder that the mind was never built to conclude.
variations: The mind is a great advisor and a terrible boss. Let it weigh in — just don't let it run the show.

items.sourceNote: Defined sacral center — the only motor that gives sustainable energy; can work tirelessly on what it loves, magnetic aura

zh-tw.content:
薦骨有定義的人，是全人類唯一一台「能持久供電」的引擎：做喜歡的事可以做到忘記時間，越做越有勁。

別人的能量會用完，你的能量是邊用邊長出來的——前提是，你得用在對的事情上。

所以你不是工作狂，你是引擎一直開著、只是常常忘記停下來。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個「投入喜歡的事就停不下來」的薦骨型朋友，提醒他這是天賦，但也記得關機。
variations: 薦骨有定義的人沒有「沒力」這個問題，只有「忘記休息」這個問題。

en.content:
If your sacral is defined, you're the only engine in the whole human lineup built for sustainable power: do what you love and you'll work till you lose track of time, gaining energy the longer you go.

Other people's energy runs out. Yours grows back as you spend it — as long as you're spending it on the right thing.

So you're not a workaholic. Your engine's just always running; you simply forget to switch it off.
en.editorNote: Send to the defined-sacral friend who can't stop once they're into something they love. It's a gift — but remind them to power down too.
variations: A defined sacral doesn't have a 'no energy' problem. It has a 'forgot to rest' problem.

items.sourceNote: Undefined sacral — no consistent life force, absorbs and amplifies others' sacral energy, overextends trying to keep up, the not-self 'I don't know when enough is enough'

zh-tw.content:
薦骨空白的人沒有自己穩定的電，卻會把別人的能量整碗吸進來、還放大——所以你常常硬撐到比有電的人還拚。

空白薦骨最大的盲點，是不知道「夠了沒」：別人累了會停，你累了還在問「我是不是不夠努力」。

你的功課不是更拚，是學會在還有力氣的時候，先收手。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「明明最累卻最不敢休息」的空白薦骨朋友，提醒他累了就是該停了。
variations: 空白薦骨的人生難題：別人的「夠了」是身體說的，你的「夠了」永遠要用想的才知道。

en.content:
An undefined sacral has no steady power of its own, yet it soaks up everyone else's energy and amplifies it — so you keep grinding harder than the people who actually have the battery for it.

The open sacral's blind spot is never knowing when enough is enough: others get tired and stop, you get tired and ask 'am I just not trying hard enough?'

Your lesson isn't to push harder. It's to pull back while you've still got something left.
en.editorNote: For the open-sacral friend who's the most worn out but the least willing to rest. Remind them: tired already means it's time to stop.
variations: The open-sacral dilemma: everyone else's 'enough' is something their body tells them. Yours, you always have to figure out in your head.

items.sourceNote: Reflector — sampling aura, canary in a coal mine for community health; signature is surprise, lives for moments of awe

zh-tw.content:
反映者像礦坑裡的金絲雀：團體健不健康，他最先有感覺——氣氛一不對，他第一個渾身不對勁。

但反映者的人生關鍵字不是「累」，是「驚喜」：當他待在對的地方、遇到真的活成自己的人，會整個亮起來，像被世界嚇到一樣開心。

所以反映者要找的不是穩定，是那些會讓他「哇」一聲的瞬間。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「對氣氛超敏感、但遇到對的場合會超發光」的反映者朋友。
variations: 反映者活著的訊號不是滿足、不是成功，是一次又一次被世界驚喜到。

en.content:
A Reflector is the canary in the coal mine: whether a group is healthy, they feel it first — the second the vibe's off, they're the first to feel all wrong.

But the keyword for a Reflector's life isn't 'tired,' it's 'surprise': put them in the right place, around people genuinely living as themselves, and they light all the way up, delighted like the world just caught them off guard.

So what a Reflector is searching for isn't stability — it's the moments that make them go 'whoa.'
en.editorNote: For the Reflector friend who's hyper-sensitive to the vibe but absolutely glows in the right setting.
variations: A Reflector's sign of being alive isn't satisfaction or success — it's getting pleasantly blindsided by the world, again and again.

items.sourceNote: Reflector — reflects whatever it samples; choosing the right place and people matters more than any decision; environment is everything

zh-tw.content:
反映者最重要的決定，從來不是「我要做什麼」，是「我要待在哪裡、跟誰一起」。

因為他會反映身邊取樣到的一切：站在對的人旁邊，他自然就好；站錯了，再怎麼努力也撐不起來。

所以給反映者的最佳建議只有一句——別急著改自己，先去找到對的地方。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合提醒那種「一直想改自己卻沒用」的反映者朋友，問題往往不在你，在你站的位置。
variations: 反映者選環境，等於別人選人生——站對地方，事情自己會對。

en.content:
A Reflector's most important decision was never 'what should I do' — it's 'where do I stand, and who am I standing with.'

Because they reflect everything they sample: next to the right people, they're naturally fine; in the wrong spot, no amount of effort holds them up.

So the single best piece of advice for a Reflector is this — don't rush to fix yourself, go find the right place first.
en.editorNote: For the Reflector friend who keeps trying to fix themselves with no result. The problem usually isn't you — it's where you're standing.
variations: For a Reflector, choosing your environment is what choosing a whole life is for everyone else — stand in the right place and things just fall into line.

items.sourceNote: 4/6 profile — opportunist (4th line, network/friendships) plus role model (6th line); three life phases, leads by lived experience not perfection

zh-tw.content:
四/六人生角色的人，前半輩子靠「人脈」吃飯：機會幾乎都是朋友、熟人介紹來的，硬投陌生履歷反而沒下文。

而那個「六」，是要等三十歲後慢慢長出來的——年輕時跌跌撞撞，中年才站上「過來人」的位置，用自己走過的路去示範給別人看。

所以四/六不是大器晚成，是設計就排好了：先交朋友、再活成榜樣。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個「工作機會都是朋友介紹、年輕時很迷惘但越老越穩」的四/六朋友。
variations: 四/六的人脈不是工具，是命脈：對的事，通常是對的人帶來的。

en.content:
If your profile is 4/6, your first act runs on your network: the good opportunities almost always come through friends and acquaintances — cold-applying to strangers tends to go nowhere.

And that '6'? It only grows in after your thirties. You stumble through the early years, then in mid-life you finally step into the 'been there' seat, modeling the way by walking it first.

So a 4/6 isn't a late bloomer — they're running the schedule as designed: make the friends first, become the role model later.
en.editorNote: Send to the 4/6 friend whose every job came through a referral, who was lost when young but only gets steadier with age.
variations: For a 4/6, your network isn't a tool — it's a lifeline. The right thing usually arrives through the right person.

items.sourceNote: 6/2 profile — role model (6th line) plus hermit/natural (2nd line); conscious drive toward wisdom, unconscious need for solitude and innate talent

zh-tw.content:
六/二人生角色，是「榜樣 × 隱士」的組合：一邊知道自己遲早要站出來當別人的標竿，一邊又超想躲在家裡誰都別來吵。

那個「二」的天賦，是別人看得到、自己卻常常無感的——你覺得「這沒什麼啊」，旁人卻一直問「你怎麼做到的」。

所以六/二不是孤僻，是需要獨處充電的天才，被對的人點名召喚時，才會心甘情願走出來。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個「明明很有才華卻很愛宅、被叫出門要三催四請」的六/二朋友。
variations: 六/二的人生節奏：在家當隱士養功力，被召喚時再出來當榜樣。

en.content:
A 6/2 profile is 'role model × hermit': one half knows you're destined to stand up as someone others look to, the other half just wants to hide at home with the door shut.

That '2' talent is the kind everyone else can see but you can't feel — you shrug 'this is nothing,' while people keep asking 'how do you even do that?'

So a 6/2 isn't antisocial. They're a genius who recharges in solitude and only comes out, willingly, when the right person calls them by name.
en.editorNote: Send to the 6/2 friend who's clearly gifted but loves staying in, and needs three reminders before they'll come out.
variations: A 6/2's rhythm: be the hermit at home building the gift, then come out as the role model when you're called.

items.sourceNote: Undefined head center — absorbs and amplifies mental pressure from others; not all your questions are yours; pressure to figure out things that don't matter

zh-tw.content:
頭腦中心空白的人，最容易為「不是自己的問題」操煩到睡不著：你接收了一整圈人的疑問、靈感、焦慮，還自動幫他們想答案。

那種「我一定要把這件事想通」的壓力，多半不是你的——是你從別人那裡吸進來、又被自己放大的。

空白頭腦的功課：不是每個冒出來的問題，都需要你回答。放著不解，也是一種解。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「睡前腦袋停不下來、一直在想有的沒的」的空白頭腦朋友，提醒他很多問題根本不用解。
variations: 空白頭腦的真相：你以為的「我在思考」，常常只是「我在幫別人的疑問加班」。

en.content:
People with an undefined head center lose the most sleep over questions that were never theirs: you soak up a whole room's worth of doubts, inspirations, and worries — and then automatically try to solve them all.

That 'I have to figure this out' pressure usually isn't yours. You absorbed it from other people and then amplified it yourself.

The open-head lesson: not every question that pops up needs you to answer it. Leaving one unsolved is its own kind of solution.
en.editorNote: For the open-head friend whose brain won't shut off at bedtime, churning over random things. A reminder that most of it doesn't need solving.
variations: The open-head truth: what you call 'thinking' is often just working overtime on someone else's questions.

items.sourceNote: Undefined ajna — mental flexibility, can see many viewpoints; not-self tries to appear certain / pin down a fixed opinion; pretends to know

zh-tw.content:
邏輯中心（Ajna）空白的人，腦袋超靈活：今天這樣想、明天那樣想，每個角度都能切換，根本不需要一個「固定答案」。

問題是社會一直逼你「要有立場、要很確定」，於是你常常硬裝出一副「我早就想清楚了」的樣子——其實那才最累。

空白邏輯的自由，是大方承認「我也還在想」。不必裝懂，是你的超能力，不是缺點。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「常被說立場不堅定、其實只是看得到很多面」的空白邏輯朋友，幫他卸下裝懂的壓力。
variations: 空白邏輯的人不是沒主見，是不想被一個答案綁死——你的彈性，是天賦不是漏洞。

en.content:
People with an undefined Ajna have wildly flexible minds: you think one way today, another tomorrow, flipping between angles freely — you genuinely don't need a single 'fixed answer.'

The trouble is society keeps demanding you 'have a stance, be certain,' so you fake a 'I had this figured out ages ago' face. And that pretending is what actually exhausts you.

The open-Ajna freedom is happily admitting 'I'm still thinking about it.' Not having to pretend you know is your superpower, not a flaw.
en.editorNote: For the open-Ajna friend who gets called wishy-washy but really just sees every side. Take the pressure to fake certainty off them.
variations: An open Ajna isn't spineless — they just refuse to get chained to one answer. Your flexibility is the gift, not the bug.

items.sourceNote: Undefined G center — no fixed sense of self/direction; shape-shifts identity and direction depending on who/where; needs the right place and people to feel found

zh-tw.content:
G 中心（自我與方向）空白的人，沒有一個固定的「我是誰」：跟不同的人在一起，你會變成不同版本的自己，連人生方向都會跟著換。

所以你常常焦慮：「為什麼別人都好像知道自己要什麼，只有我一直在飄？」

但這不是迷失，是你的設計——你不是要「找到」一個永遠的方向，是要「待對地方」，讓對的環境帶你走到對的路上。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「常覺得自己沒方向、很羨慕目標明確的人」的空白 G 朋友，提醒他方向是環境給的，不是想出來的。
variations: 空白 G 的人生不是「找到自己」，是「站對地方」——位置對了，你自然就知道往哪走。

en.content:
People with an undefined G center (self and direction) don't carry a fixed 'who I am': around different people you become different versions of yourself, and even your life direction shifts to match.

So you keep feeling anxious — 'why does everyone else seem to know what they want, while I just drift?'

But that's not being lost, it's your design. You're not meant to 'find' one permanent direction — you're meant to be in the right place, and let the right environment carry you onto the right path.
en.editorNote: For the open-G friend who feels directionless and envies people with clear goals. A reminder that direction comes from where you stand, not from overthinking.
variations: An open-G life isn't about 'finding yourself' — it's about standing in the right place. Get the spot right and you'll just know which way to go.

items.sourceNote: Defined throat center — consistent voice/way of expressing; reliable communication style; the only manifestation center turning inner world into outer action

zh-tw.content:
喉嚨中心有定義的人，講話有一種「固定的腔調」：朋友只要聽你開口，就知道是你——那個語氣、那個節奏，是你天生的招牌。

喉嚨是全人類圖唯一的「顯化中心」，把腦袋裡的東西真正吐出來、變成行動。所以你說出口的話，比你想像中更有重量。

別人羨慕你「表達很穩」，那不是練出來的，是你出廠就裝好的。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個「講話超有個人風格、一聽就知道是他」的喉嚨有定義朋友，幫他認領這個天賦。
variations: 喉嚨有定義的人，連沉默都有自己的味道——你的聲音本身就是一個簽名檔。

en.content:
If your throat center is defined, you've got a 'fixed cadence': friends only have to hear you open your mouth to know it's you — that tone, that rhythm, is your built-in signature.

The throat is the only manifestation center in the whole chart, the place that turns what's in your head into something out in the world. So the words you say carry more weight than you realize.

People envy how 'steady' you are when you express yourself. That's not practiced — it came pre-installed.
en.editorNote: Send to the defined-throat friend with such a distinct way of talking you'd know it anywhere. Help them claim the gift.
variations: With a defined throat, even your silence has a flavor — your voice itself is a signature line.

items.sourceNote: Undefined throat — flexible expression, can speak the way that fits the moment; not-self craves attention/talks to be seen; designed to wait to be recognized or asked

zh-tw.content:
喉嚨中心空白的人，表達超彈性：可以講得很正式、也可以很搞笑，看當下需要什麼就變什麼——這是天賦。

但空白喉嚨有個陷阱：太想「被看見」，所以常常硬搶話、刻意製造聲量，講完才覺得空虛。

你的設計其實是「等被問、被點名才開口」——不是叫你閉嘴，是說對的時機自然會來，不用搶。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「聚會裡很想講話、但搶著講完又覺得不對勁」的空白喉嚨朋友，提醒他等被問更有力量。
variations: 空白喉嚨的功課：不是「我有沒有講」，是「我是不是被需要時才講」。

en.content:
People with an undefined throat are wildly flexible at expressing themselves: you can go formal, go funny, shape-shift to whatever the moment needs — that's the gift.

But the open throat has a trap: wanting so badly to be seen that you cut in, manufacture volume, talk to fill the space — and feel hollow afterward.

Your actual design is to speak once you're asked or recognized. That's not 'shut up' — it's that the right moment arrives on its own, so you don't have to grab for it.
en.editorNote: For the open-throat friend who's itching to talk at a gathering but feels off after jumping in. Waiting to be asked carries more power.
variations: The open-throat lesson isn't 'did I get to talk' — it's 'did I talk because I was actually needed.'

items.sourceNote: Undefined solar plexus — strongest conditioning center; amplifies others' emotions; not-self avoids confrontation and people-pleases to manage emotional temperature

zh-tw.content:
情緒中心空白的人，是全人類圖「最容易被制約」的一群：你會把別人的情緒整碗吸進來、還放大好幾倍。

所以你超怕衝突——不是膽小，是吵架那股情緒的強度，在你身體裡真的痛到受不了。為了讓場面別爆炸，你常常委屈自己去討好。

但討好不是和平，是你在硬扛全場的情緒。學會問一句「這情緒是我的，還是我接收來的？」，你會輕鬆一大半。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「超怕別人不開心、總是先道歉的人」，提醒他那份敏感不是缺點，是空白情緒中心在運作。
variations: 空白情緒中心的真相：你不是玻璃心，你是把整個房間的情緒都接收進來了。

en.content:
People with an undefined solar plexus are the most easily conditioned group in the whole chart: you soak up everyone else's emotions and crank them up several notches.

So you dread confrontation — not because you're timid, but because the sheer intensity of a fight genuinely hurts in your body. To keep the room from blowing up, you shrink yourself and people-please.

But people-pleasing isn't peace — it's you absorbing the whole room's emotions. Learn to ask 'is this feeling mine, or did I just pick it up?' and half the weight lifts.
en.editorNote: For the friend who's terrified of upsetting anyone and apologizes first. That sensitivity isn't a flaw — it's an open solar plexus at work.
variations: The open-solar-plexus truth: you're not 'too sensitive,' you just absorbed the emotions of the entire room.

items.sourceNote: Undefined heart/ego/will center — ~63% have it open; not-self trapped in proving worthiness, easily conditioned by self-improvement messaging; nothing to prove

zh-tw.content:
意志力中心（Heart/Ego）空白的人——其實佔了全世界六成多——一輩子最大的陷阱，是「不停證明自己」。

你接收了別人的意志力，誤以為「我也得很有毅力、很拚、很值得」，結果一直追著「夠不夠好」這個問題跑，怎麼追都追不完。

但空白意志力的真相剛好相反：你根本沒有什麼要證明的。把那股借來的壓力放下，你會發現，你的價值不用靠成就來換。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「總覺得要做出點成績才配得上」的空白意志力朋友，提醒他那份「不夠」的感覺是借來的。
variations: 空白意志力的解脫：當你不再想證明自己，你才發現自己一直都夠了。

en.content:
People with an undefined heart (ego/will) center — and that's over 60% of the planet — spend a lifetime caught in one trap: endlessly proving themselves.

You take in other people's willpower and mistake it for 'I have to be relentless, hustle harder, earn my worth,' so you chase the question 'am I good enough?' forever and never catch it.

But the open-will truth is the exact opposite: you have nothing to prove. Set down that borrowed pressure and you'll find your worth was never something to be traded for achievements.
en.editorNote: For the open-will friend who feels they have to produce results to deserve their place. Remind them that 'not enough' feeling is borrowed.
variations: The open-will liberation: the moment you stop trying to prove yourself, you realize you were always already enough.

items.sourceNote: Defined heart/ego/will center — consistent willpower; can make and keep promises; needs to honor commitments and rest; should only promise what it genuinely wants

zh-tw.content:
意志力中心有定義的人，是少數「說到做到」的引擎：你答應的事會做到底，意志力穩定到別人都靠你撐。

但這也是你的陷阱——因為你做得到，所以容易亂答應，最後把自己壓垮。你的意志力是有檔期的，用完需要真正休息，不是靠咬牙硬撐。

所以你的功課不是「更努力遵守承諾」，是「只答應你真心想做的事」。守住這條線，你的說到做到才珍貴。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個「答應的事一定做到、結果常常被各種承諾壓垮」的意志力有定義朋友，提醒他承諾前先過濾。
variations: 意志力有定義的人最該學的不是堅持，是拒絕：別讓「我做得到」變成「我什麼都答應」。

en.content:
People with a defined heart (ego/will) center are the rare engines that actually 'say it and do it': what you promise, you follow through on, with willpower steady enough that everyone leans on you.

But that's also your trap — because you can deliver, you over-promise and crush yourself under it. Your willpower runs on a schedule; when it's spent it needs real rest, not gritted teeth.

So your lesson isn't 'keep your promises harder' — it's 'only promise what you genuinely want to do.' Hold that line, and your word becomes truly valuable.
en.editorNote: Send to the defined-will friend who always delivers but gets buried under everything they agreed to. Remind them to filter before promising.
variations: The hardest thing for a defined-will person to learn isn't perseverance — it's saying no. Don't let 'I can do it' turn into 'I'll do everything.'

items.sourceNote: Undefined root center — pressure center that absorbs and amplifies others' urgency; not-self rushes to relieve pressure, starts things just to look busy; learning to discern what's actually urgent

zh-tw.content:
根中心空白的人，像一塊海綿：把周圍所有人的急迫感整碗吸進來、還放大——所以你常常莫名其妙覺得「好像有一堆事在催我，但又說不出在催什麼」。

空白根最大的陷阱，是「為了擺脫壓力而衝」：草草把事做完只為了鬆一口氣，結果做完一件又自己生出下一件，永遠停不下來。

但真相是，那股壓力多半不是你的。你的天賦反而是——看清楚哪些事真的急，哪些只是被你接收進來的雜訊。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「總覺得被一堆事追著跑、停下來就焦慮」的空白根朋友，提醒他那份急多半是借來的。
variations: 空白根的功課：分清楚「這件事真的急」跟「我只是急著想擺脫這股壓力」——後者會騙你做一堆其實不必做的事。

en.content:
People with an undefined root center are like a sponge: you soak up everyone else's urgency and crank it up — so you keep feeling, for no clear reason, like 'there's a pile of stuff rushing me, but I can't even say what.'

The open root's big trap is rushing just to relieve the pressure: slapping a task done only to feel that momentary breath of relief, then immediately spawning the next one, never able to stop.

But the truth is, most of that pressure was never yours. Your real gift is the opposite — seeing clearly which things are genuinely urgent and which are just noise you absorbed.
en.editorNote: For the open-root friend who always feels chased by a to-do pile and gets anxious the moment they stop. Most of that rush is borrowed.
variations: The open-root lesson: tell 'this is genuinely urgent' apart from 'I'm just rushing to get out from under the pressure.' The second one tricks you into doing a pile of things you never needed to.

items.sourceNote: Defined G center — ~57% of people; fixed, consistent sense of identity and direction; a quiet background stability like a ship's keel; knows who they are even when the surface is choppy

zh-tw.content:
G 中心（自我與方向）有定義的人，內建一個穩定的「我是誰」：就算外在再亂、再迷惘，骨子裡你大概知道自己是什麼樣的人、人生要往哪個方向走。

這不是固執，是像船的龍骨——海面再怎麼晃，你不會翻。

所以別人會莫名想靠近你、覺得跟你在一起很安心，因為你的方向感本身就是一種定錨。你不必一直找自己，你只要記得：方向本來就在你身上。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個「不管多亂都還是很有自己樣子、朋友都愛找他拿主意」的 G 有定義朋友，幫他認領這份穩。
variations: G 有定義的人不是不會迷路，是迷路時心裡還有一根指南針——晃歸晃，方向不會丟。

en.content:
People with a defined G center (self and direction) come with a stable built-in 'who I am': however messy or lost things get on the outside, deep down you roughly know what kind of person you are and which way your life is headed.

That's not stubbornness — it's like a ship's keel. The surface can churn all it wants; you don't capsize.

So people are drawn to you without quite knowing why, and feel safe around you — your sense of direction is itself an anchor. You don't have to keep searching for yourself. Just remember: the direction was always inside you.
en.editorNote: Send to the defined-G friend who stays unmistakably themselves no matter the chaos and is everyone's go-to for advice. Help them claim that steadiness.
variations: A defined G doesn't never get lost — they just keep a compass inside while they're lost. The surface rocks; the direction doesn't go missing.

items.sourceNote: Defined emotional solar plexus — ~50% of people; consistent emotional wave with highs and lows, sometimes for no reason; moods are normal, not instability; carries emotional energy at all times

zh-tw.content:
情緒中心有定義的人，全世界大約一半——你身上隨時都掛著一道「情緒波」，有時高有時低，常常還找不到原因。

這不是你不穩定，是你的設計就長這樣：情緒像潮汐，自己會漲會退。今天莫名很 down、明天又莫名很嗨，都正常到不行。

所以你最該學的，不是「控制情緒」，是「別在浪頭上做決定」——等波走完，那個平靜下來的你，才看得清真正想要什麼。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「常被說情緒化、自己也搞不懂為什麼今天就是不對勁」的情緒型朋友，幫他正名：那是天生的波，不是壞掉。
variations: 情緒有定義的人沒有「無緣無故心情差」這回事，只有「波還沒走完」這回事。

en.content:
People with a defined emotional solar plexus — about half the planet — carry an 'emotional wave' with them at all times: some days up, some days down, often for no findable reason.

That's not you being unstable; it's literally how you're built. Your emotions move like the tide — they rise and fall on their own. Inexplicably low today, inexplicably buzzing tomorrow: completely, utterly normal.

So the thing to learn isn't 'control your emotions' — it's 'don't decide on the crest of a wave.' Let it run its course, and the calmer you on the other side is the one who can actually see what you want.
en.editorNote: For the emotional-type friend who gets called moody and can't figure out why today just feels off. Reframe it: that's a natural wave, not a malfunction.
variations: An emotional type doesn't have a 'bad mood out of nowhere' — they have a 'wave that hasn't finished yet.'

items.sourceNote: Emotional authority — no truth in the now, clarity only comes over time; waiting through the wave is the superpower, never decide at the peak (high or low)

zh-tw.content:
情緒型權威最該記住的一句話：當下，沒有真相。

你在情緒高點答應的事、在情緒低點放棄的事，常常都不是你真正要的——那只是浪的最高或最低點，不是你。

等一等，讓波慢慢走完，會浮上來一種叫「情緒清明」的東西：不是更興奮、也不是更厭世，就是一種「嗯，這件事可以」的平靜。等到那個平靜，再點頭也不遲。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「興頭上答應、冷靜後超後悔」的情緒型朋友，提醒他重要的事都值得睡一覺再決定。
variations: 情緒型的人做決定不是看「我現在想不想」，是看「等情緒平了，我還想不想」。

en.content:
The one line every emotional authority should tattoo on their brain: there's no truth in the now.

The thing you said yes to on a high, the thing you bailed on at a low — usually neither was what you actually wanted. That was just the crest or the trough of the wave, not you.

Wait. Let the wave run all the way out, and something called 'emotional clarity' surfaces: not more excited, not more over-it, just a quiet 'yeah, this one's good.' Wait for that calm, then nod. It won't be too late.
en.editorNote: For the emotional-type friend who says yes in the heat of the moment and deeply regrets it once they cool off. Anything that matters is worth sleeping on.
variations: An emotional type decides not by 'do I want this right now' but by 'once the feelings settle, do I still want it.'

items.sourceNote: Undefined head center — pressure to answer everyone's questions and solve problems that aren't yours; not-self absorbs and amplifies mental pressure; learning that not every question needs your answer

zh-tw.content:
頭腦中心空白的人，最容易被一件事拖垮：想幫全世界解答問題。

別人隨口一句「這該怎麼辦」，你就整晚睡不著幫他想；那些問題其實根本不是你的，你卻整碗接下來、還放大到失眠。

空白頭腦的真相是——不是每個冒出來的問題都需要你回答，也不是每個念頭都值得你追下去。學會讓問題只是問題，你的腦袋才有空。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「別人一個問題就想到天亮、明明不關他的事」的空白頭腦朋友，提醒他不必當全人類的解答機。
variations: 空白頭腦的功課：分清楚「這是我真心想懂的」跟「這只是別人丟過來、我順手接住的」——後者放掉，你會清醒很多。

en.content:
People with an undefined head center get worn down by one thing above all: trying to answer everyone's questions.

Someone tosses out an offhand 'what should I do about this,' and you lie awake all night solving it for them. Those questions were never yours — you just scooped them up whole and amplified them into insomnia.

The open-head truth: not every question that pops up needs your answer, and not every thought is worth chasing down. Let a question just be a question, and your head finally gets some room.
en.editorNote: For the open-head friend who stays up till dawn over a problem that was never even theirs. They don't have to be humanity's answer machine.
variations: The open-head lesson: tell 'this is something I genuinely want to understand' apart from 'this is just something someone handed me and I caught on reflex.' Drop the second one and you'll think so much clearer.

items.sourceNote: Defined head center — fixed, consistent way of being inspired and generating questions; a reliable source of mental pressure and inspiration for others; not meant to answer all its own questions either

zh-tw.content:
頭腦中心有定義的人，是一台停不下來的「問題產生器」：靈感和疑問一個接一個冒出來，連睡前都在想「欸那如果⋯⋯」。

這是你的天賦——你會拋出讓一群人眼睛一亮的好問題，帶著大家一起想。

但別掉進一個坑：以為每個問題都得由你自己找到答案。你的角色是「提出」，不是「解完」。問題丟出去，剩下的可以交給世界。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個「腦袋轉不停、金句問題一堆、卻常常被自己的思緒壓垮」的定義頭腦朋友，提醒他發問是天賦、不是作業。
variations: 定義頭腦的人最該放下的執念：不是每個你想到的問題，都欠你一個答案。

en.content:
People with a defined head center are a question-generator that never powers down: inspiration and questions keep bubbling up, one after another, and even at bedtime you're going 'wait, but what if…'

That's your gift — you throw out the kind of question that makes a whole room light up and think alongside you.

But don't fall into the trap of believing every question is yours to answer. Your role is to raise them, not to solve them all. Put the question out there, and the rest you can leave to the world.
en.editorNote: Send to the defined-head friend whose brain never stops, who's full of great questions but gets crushed under their own thoughts. Asking is the gift — it's not homework.
variations: The obsession a defined-head person most needs to drop: not every question you dream up owes you an answer.

items.sourceNote: Undefined ajna center — flexible, adaptable thinking; not-self pretends to be certain of things it isn't sure about; the gift is holding many perspectives without needing one fixed truth

zh-tw.content:
邏輯中心（Ajna）空白的人，腦袋是「彈性」的：今天這樣想、明天那樣想，能同時裝下好幾種完全相反的觀點，還都覺得有道理。

但空白 Ajna 有個超常見的陷阱——假裝很篤定。因為怕被說「你怎麼沒個定見」，你會硬撐出一個其實自己也不確定的答案。

其實「我還沒想清楚」不是弱點，是你的天賦。你本來就不必只信一種答案，開放和流動，才是你最厲害的地方。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「常被說沒主見、其實是想法太多元」的空白 Ajna 朋友，提醒他「我不確定」講出來一點都不丟臉。
variations: 空白 Ajna 的解脫：你不用假裝很確定。承認「我還在想」，反而比硬給一個答案更接近真的你。

en.content:
People with an undefined ajna center have a flexible mind: you think one way today, another tomorrow, and can hold several completely opposite views at once — all of them feeling reasonable.

But the open ajna has a very common trap: faking certainty. Scared of being told 'don't you have any opinions of your own,' you'll force out an answer you're not actually sure of.

Here's the thing — 'I haven't figured it out yet' isn't a weakness, it's your gift. You were never meant to commit to one fixed truth. Staying open and fluid is where you're strongest.
en.editorNote: For the open-ajna friend who gets called wishy-washy but really just holds too many perspectives at once. Saying 'I'm not sure' is nothing to be ashamed of.
variations: The open-ajna liberation: you don't have to fake certainty. Admitting 'I'm still thinking' gets you closer to the real you than forcing out an answer ever will.

items.sourceNote: Defined ajna center — fixed, consistent way of conceptualizing; mental certainty that can tip into rigidity; strength is a reliable framework, shadow is struggling to stay open to new perspectives

zh-tw.content:
邏輯中心（Ajna）有定義的人，腦袋有一套固定的作業系統：想事情有自己的框架，觀點穩定，講出來的道理總是有條有理。

這讓你顯得很可靠、很有想法——但也藏著一個陷阱：太確定，容易變成太固執，別人一提新角度你就下意識想反駁。

你的框架是天賦，不是牢籠。偶爾鬆一下手，讓不同的想法先進來坐一下，你的清晰才不會變成死角。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個「講話超有邏輯、但一旦認定就很難被說服」的定義 Ajna 朋友，溫柔提醒他留一點門縫。
variations: 定義 Ajna 的功課：你的想法很清楚是好事，但「清楚」跟「聽不進別人」只差一線。

en.content:
People with a defined ajna center run on a fixed mental operating system: you have your own framework for thinking, your views hold steady, and your reasoning always comes out neat and structured.

It makes you look reliable and sharp — but there's a hidden trap: too certain tips easily into too rigid, and the second someone offers a new angle, you instinctively want to argue it down.

Your framework is a gift, not a cage. Loosen your grip now and then, let a different idea come in and sit for a while, and your clarity won't harden into a blind spot.
en.editorNote: Send to the defined-ajna friend whose logic is airtight but who's near-impossible to sway once they've decided. A gentle nudge to leave the door cracked.
variations: The defined-ajna lesson: having clear views is great — but 'clear' and 'can't hear anyone else' are only a hair apart.

items.sourceNote: Undefined throat center — flexible, adaptable communication style that shifts with people/environment; not-self talks too much or forces attention to get seen; gift is connecting with a wide range of voices

zh-tw.content:
喉嚨中心空白的人，講話會「隨環境變」：跟這群人一個樣、跟那群人又是另一個樣，連語氣、用詞都會自動切換。

這不是你人格分裂，是你天生就能接住各種頻率的人。但空白喉嚨有個坑——怕沒被聽見，就會拚命講、搶著開口、硬要引人注意，結果講越多越心虛。

你的表達力不是靠「講最多」，是靠「等對的時機」。被邀請、被點名的時候再開口，你的話反而最有份量。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「在不同場合像不同人、又常怕安靜下來沒人注意」的空白喉嚨朋友，提醒他不用搶話，等被邀請就好。
variations: 空白喉嚨的功課：不是每個安靜都要你去填。與其搶著講，不如等那個「換你說」的時刻。

en.content:
People with an undefined throat center shift how they talk depending on the room: one way with this crowd, another way with that one — even tone and word choice switch automatically.

That's not a split personality; you're just wired to meet people on all kinds of frequencies. But the open throat has a trap — afraid of going unheard, you overtalk, jump in first, force the spotlight, and the more you say the more hollow it feels.

Your voice doesn't land by 'saying the most' — it lands by 'waiting for the right moment.' Speak when you're invited or called on, and your words carry the most weight of anyone's.
en.editorNote: For the open-throat friend who's a different person in every setting and dreads a silence where no one's looking at them. No need to grab the mic — just wait to be invited.
variations: The open-throat lesson: not every silence is yours to fill. Instead of racing to speak, wait for the moment that's actually 'your turn.'

items.sourceNote: Defined G center — fixed, consistent sense of identity and direction; knows who they are, trusts their trajectory even without a map; the magnetic monopole pulls them along when they follow strategy and authority

zh-tw.content:
G 中心（身份中心）有定義的人，自帶一顆「內建指南針」：不管外面怎麼變，你心裡總有一個穩穩的「我就是我」。

就算暫時看不到路，你也隱約知道自己正往對的方向走——不是因為想通了，是身體裡那股方向感從沒斷過。

所以別人問你「你到底想做什麼」，你可能講不清楚，但你就是知道「這個對、那個不對」。那不是固執，是你的方向感在說話。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個「說不太出人生規劃、但莫名很清楚自己是誰」的定義 G 朋友，那份篤定是天賦。
variations: 定義 G 的人不一定有地圖，但永遠有指南針：路可以慢慢找，方向從來不用問別人。

en.content:
People with a defined G center (the identity center) come with a built-in compass: no matter how much the outside world shifts, there's a steady 'I am who I am' sitting inside you.

Even when you can't see the road, you vaguely know you're headed the right way — not because you've reasoned it out, but because that sense of direction in your body never actually cuts out.

So when people ask 'what do you even want to do,' you might not be able to spell it out, yet you just know 'this one's right, that one's not.' That's not stubbornness — that's your inner direction talking.
en.editorNote: Send to the defined-G friend who can't quite articulate a life plan but is somehow crystal clear on who they are. That certainty is a gift.
variations: A defined-G person doesn't always have a map, but they always have a compass: the road you can find slowly, but the direction you never have to ask anyone for.

items.sourceNote: Undefined G center — fluid identity that shifts with people and place; not-self keeps searching for a fixed self and the right direction, chases love; gift is adaptability and finding the right environment matters more than forcing an identity

zh-tw.content:
G 中心空白的人，身份是「流動」的：跟不同的人在一起，你會活出不同版本的自己，連「我是誰」都會隨著場合換一套。

所以你常常焦慮：「我到底是什麼樣的人？我的人生方向在哪？」——但這個問題本來就不該用「想」的來逼出答案。

空白 G 的秘密不在你身上，在你「待在哪裡、跟誰一起」。站對地方、遇對的人，那個對的你和對的方向，自己就會浮出來。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「一直在找自己、換個環境就像換個人」的空白 G 朋友，提醒他別急著定義自己，先找對位置。
variations: 空白 G 的功課：不用逼自己「決定我是誰」，你的身份跟方向，是被對的環境「照」出來的，不是被想出來的。

en.content:
People with an undefined G center have a fluid identity: around different people you live out different versions of yourself, and even 'who am I' swaps outfits depending on the room.

So you keep getting anxious: 'what kind of person am I, really? Where's my direction in life?' — but that question was never meant to be forced into an answer by thinking harder.

The open-G secret isn't inside you — it's in 'where you are and who you're with.' Stand in the right place, around the right people, and the right you and the right direction surface on their own.
en.editorNote: For the open-G friend who's forever searching for themselves and turns into a different person in every new setting. Stop rushing to define yourself — find the right place first.
variations: The open-G lesson: you don't have to force yourself to 'decide who I am.' Your identity and direction get reflected out of you by the right environment — they're not something you think your way into.

items.sourceNote: Undefined root center — pressure center; absorbs and amplifies others' stress and urgency, rushes to clear the to-do list to relieve pressure that isn't theirs; learning that the pressure to hurry is borrowed and there's no real rush

zh-tw.content:
根中心空白的人，最容易被一種「莫名的急」追著跑：明明沒人逼，你卻總覺得「快點做完、快點解決，不然壓力好大」。

真相是——那股急迫感常常不是你的，是你從周遭一整圈焦慮的人身上吸進來、還放大的。你以為在趕自己的進度，其實在幫別人的壓力加班。

空白根的功課：先看清楚「這件事真的急嗎」。很多時候，只要你不急著把壓力清掉，它自己就會消風——世界沒有你以為的那麼趕。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「永遠像在趕、清完一件又找下一件來壓自己」的空白根朋友，提醒他那份急多半是借來的。
variations: 空白根的解脫：不是每個「快點」都是你的。先分清楚「我真的趕時間」跟「我只是接到了別人的急」。

en.content:
People with an undefined root center get chased around by a nameless sense of rush: nobody's actually pushing you, yet you constantly feel 'hurry up and finish, hurry up and fix it, or the pressure's unbearable.'

The truth is — that urgency usually isn't yours. You soaked it up from a whole circle of anxious people and then amplified it. You think you're racing your own to-do list, but really you're working overtime on other people's stress.

The open-root lesson: first check whether the thing is actually urgent. A lot of the time, the moment you stop rushing to clear the pressure, it deflates on its own — the world isn't nearly as much of a hurry as you think.
en.editorNote: For the open-root friend who always seems to be racing, clearing one thing just to pile on the next. That rush is mostly borrowed.
variations: The open-root release: not every 'hurry up' is yours. First tell 'I'm genuinely on a deadline' apart from 'I just caught someone else's urgency.'

items.sourceNote: Defined heart/ego/will center — consistent willpower and self-worth; can make and keep promises, thrives on commitments it genuinely chooses; not meant to prove its worth, only about a third of people have this defined

zh-tw.content:
意志力中心（Heart／Ego）有定義的人，是全世界少數自帶「說到做到」引擎的人：你答應的事，身體真的會生出力氣去完成，而且做到會有種踏實的爽感。

只有大約三分之一的人有這個定義，所以別拿你的標準去要求別人——不是每個人都能像你這樣「靠意志力硬扛」，那對他們是會爆掉的。

你的功課是：把承諾留給你「真心想要」的事。你的意志力很寶貴，別浪費在為了證明自己而答應的空頭支票上。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個「答應了就一定做到、但常常對別人的半途而廢很不諒解」的定義意志力朋友，提醒他那是天賦、不是標配。
variations: 定義意志力的人最該記得：你的「說到做到」是天賦，不是別人欠你的義務。把力氣留給真的想要的事。

en.content:
People with a defined heart center (the ego/willpower center) are among the rare few born with a 'say it, do it' engine: when you promise something, your body genuinely generates the drive to follow through, and finishing gives you a solid, satisfying kick.

Only about a third of people have this defined, so don't hold everyone else to your standard — not everyone can 'muscle through on willpower' like you do; for them it burns out fast.

Your lesson: save your commitments for the things you actually want. Your willpower is precious — don't waste it on empty promises made just to prove yourself.
en.editorNote: Send to the defined-heart friend who always delivers on a promise but takes others' flakiness personally. It's their gift, not the standard-issue setting.
variations: The one thing a defined-heart person should remember: your 'say it, do it' is a gift, not a debt everyone else owes you. Save the willpower for what you truly want.

items.sourceNote: Undefined heart/ego/will center — inconsistent willpower; not-self tries to prove its worth and overpromises to feel valued, guilt over broken commitments; learning self-worth isn't something you have to earn through willpower

zh-tw.content:
意志力中心空白的人，最常掉進一個坑：拚命想「證明自己夠好」。你會不自覺答應太多、扛太多、把自己逼到極限，只為了讓人覺得你有價值。

但空白意志力沒有穩定的「硬撐力」，所以那些咬牙答應的事常常做不完，然後你又陷進「我怎麼這麼廢」的罪惡感裡——這根本是一個沒有終點的迴圈。

真相是：你的價值，從來不需要靠意志力去掙。你本來就夠好了，不用再拿承諾去買別人的肯定。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「老是答應太多、做不到又狂自責」的空白意志力朋友，提醒他不用再證明什麼了。
variations: 空白意志力的解脫：你不用靠「硬撐做到」來換價值。停止跟自己較勁，你會發現你早就夠好了。

en.content:
People with an undefined heart center fall into one trap above all: desperately trying to 'prove they're good enough.' You unconsciously say yes to too much, shoulder too much, push yourself to the limit — all so people will see you as valuable.

But an open heart has no steady 'muscle through' power, so all those gritted-teeth promises often go unfinished, and then you sink into 'why am I so useless' guilt — a loop with no finish line.

The truth: your worth was never something you have to earn through willpower. You're already enough — you don't need to keep buying other people's approval with promises.
en.editorNote: For the open-heart friend who overcommits, can't deliver, and then beats themselves up. A reminder that they have nothing left to prove.
variations: The open-heart release: you don't have to 'muscle it through' to be worth something. Stop wrestling with yourself and you'll see you were already enough.

items.sourceNote: Defined spleen center — consistent, reliable intuition and instinct in the present moment; a steady internal 'security system'; the hit feels the same every time so it can be trusted

zh-tw.content:
脾中心有定義的人，自帶一套「穩定的直覺雷達」：那個一閃而過的「不行、快閃」或「這個可以」，每次的感覺都長一樣，久了你就知道那是真的。

因為它穩定，所以可靠——你的身體會在當下、事情發生的那一秒，就給你一個乾淨的答案，不囉唆、不糾結。

所以別再用腦袋去覆蓋它了。那個瞬間的直覺不是想太多，是你出廠就裝好的保全系統在報平安。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個「第一直覺永遠最準、卻常常想一想又推翻自己」的定義脾朋友，提醒他那台雷達值得信。
variations: 定義脾的人不缺直覺，缺的是「相信第一秒那個感覺」的勇氣。

en.content:
If your spleen is defined, you come with a steady intuition radar: that flash of 'nope, get out' or 'yeah, this one's fine' feels the same every single time — and once you notice that, you know it's real.

Because it's consistent, it's reliable. Your body hands you a clean answer right in the moment, the second something happens — no fuss, no spiral.

So stop letting your head override it. That split-second instinct isn't overthinking — it's the security system you shipped with, checking in that all's clear.
en.editorNote: Send to the defined-spleen friend whose first instinct is always right but who talks themselves out of it. That radar is worth trusting.
variations: A defined spleen isn't short on intuition — it's short on the nerve to trust that very first-second feeling.

items.sourceNote: Electromagnetic connection — one person holds one gate of a channel, the other holds the matching gate; the channel completes only when they meet, creating instant magnetism and energy neither had alone

zh-tw.content:
人類圖裡有一種「電磁吸引」：你身上有半條通道，剛好某個人身上有另外半條——你們一碰面，那條通道就接通了，瞬間來電。

所以有些人你一見面就莫名其妙被吸住，不是命中注定那麼玄，是你們合起來剛好補齊了一段彼此都缺的能量。

那股火花很迷人，但別忘了：那是「你們一起」才有的東西。對方一離開，那半條通道也跟著走——所以它讓人上癮，也讓人患得患失。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「遇到某個人就莫名來電、分開又特別失落」的朋友，幫他看懂那股吸引力的機制。
variations: 電磁吸引最迷人也最危險的地方：那份「完整」是靠對方補上的，不是你本來就有的。

en.content:
Human Design has a thing called electromagnetic attraction: you carry half of a channel, and someone happens to carry the other half — the moment you meet, the channel snaps shut and there's an instant spark.

So the person you're inexplicably drawn to on sight? It's not fate being mysterious — it's the two of you completing a piece of energy each of you was missing.

That spark is intoxicating, but remember: it only exists 'when you're together.' The second they leave, their half of the channel goes with them — which is exactly why it's so addictive, and so easy to feel needy about.
en.editorNote: For the friend who feels an instant pull toward someone and a deep dip when they're apart. Help them see the mechanics behind the magnetism.
variations: The most intoxicating and most dangerous thing about electromagnetic attraction: the 'wholeness' is something they complete in you — it was never yours to begin with.

items.sourceNote: Generator vs Manifesting Generator — pure Generator is a marathon runner who masters one thing deeply, MG is a sprinter who goes 'inch deep, mile wide'; both share sacral response and satisfaction/frustration

zh-tw.content:
同樣有薦骨，純生產者跟顯示生產者的差別，是「馬拉松選手」跟「短跑衝刺者」。

純生產者喜歡一頭栽進一件事，慢慢磨、越挖越深，最後練成一身絕活；顯示生產者則是「一寸深、一哩廣」，同時碰十件事，快速換手。

所以別拿同一把尺量他們：逼生產者一直換跑道，他會煩；逼顯示生產者只准專精一件事，他會窒息。兩個都對，只是節奏天生不一樣。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「一個朋友超專一、一個朋友超跳躍」的組合，幫兩邊互相理解對方的節奏。
variations: 純生產者往下鑽，顯示生產者往旁邊擴——沒有誰比較好，只是一個練深度、一個練廣度。

en.content:
Same sacral, but the difference between a pure Generator and a Manifesting Generator is a marathon runner versus a sprinter.

A pure Generator loves to dive into one thing, grind at it, dig deeper and deeper until they've mastered a real craft. A Manifesting Generator is 'inch deep, mile wide' — juggling ten things at once, switching fast.

So don't measure them with the same ruler: force a Generator to keep changing lanes and they get annoyed; force a Manifesting Generator to specialize in one thing and they suffocate. Both are right — the rhythm is just wired differently.
en.editorNote: For the duo where one friend is laser-focused and the other bounces everywhere. Help each understand the other's tempo.
variations: A pure Generator drills down, a Manifesting Generator spreads out — neither's better, one just builds depth and the other builds range.

items.sourceNote: Manifesting Generator burnout — doesn't look like collapse; looks like running on adrenaline instead of sacral energy, pushing with mental willpower, mistaking busyness for alignment; root cause is overcommitting from a mental yes

zh-tw.content:
顯示生產者的過勞，長得跟別人不一樣：它不是癱倒、不是關機，而是「用腎上腺素硬撐、把忙碌誤當成充實」。

危險就在這裡——你看起來還在飛速前進，其實燒的不是薦骨那股真正的動力，是靠意志力在硬推，撐到最後只剩焦躁跟一肚子火。

幾乎所有顯示生產者的爆掉，源頭都是同一件事：用腦袋答應了薦骨根本不想做的事。所以累不是問題，「用腦袋答應」才是。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「明明超忙卻越忙越煩躁」的顯示生產者朋友，提醒他停下來問薦骨：這件事我真的想做嗎？
variations: 顯示生產者的過勞不會讓你倒下，會讓你一邊高速運轉、一邊莫名其妙火大——那就是薦骨在抗議了。

en.content:
A Manifesting Generator's burnout doesn't look like anyone else's: it's not a collapse, not a shutdown — it's running on adrenaline and mistaking busyness for fulfillment.

That's exactly where it's dangerous. You look like you're still flying, but what you're burning isn't real sacral fuel — it's mental willpower shoving you forward, until all that's left is restlessness and a low, simmering anger.

Nearly every MG meltdown traces back to the same thing: your head said yes to something your sacral never wanted. So being tired isn't the problem — the 'yes from your head' is.
en.editorNote: For the Manifesting Generator friend who's slammed busy but getting crankier by the day. A nudge to stop and ask the sacral: do I actually want this?
variations: An MG's burnout won't knock you down — it'll have you sprinting at full speed and inexplicably furious. That's the sacral filing a complaint.

items.sourceNote: Undefined spleen — absorbs and amplifies others' fears, so most fears felt are not one's own; splenic fear moves through action; learning to distinguish borrowed fear from real intuition

zh-tw.content:
脾中心空白的人，最容易被「不是自己的恐懼」綁架：你會把周圍所有人的怕整碗吸進來、還放大——所以那個讓你半夜驚醒的擔憂，很可能根本不是你的。

真正的直覺很安靜、只說一次；借來的恐懼很吵、會一直跟你耗、逼你什麼都不敢動。分不清這兩個，你會一輩子被莫名的怕困在原地。

有趣的是，脾型的恐懼要靠「行動」才會散——你越是站著怕，它越大；真的踏出去做了，才發現：欸，也還好嘛。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「常常莫名很焦慮、卻說不出在怕什麼」的空白脾朋友，提醒他先問：這份怕，是我的嗎？
variations: 空白脾的功課：分清楚「這是直覺在提醒我」跟「這只是我接收來、又放大的別人的怕」——後者，動起來就散了。

en.content:
People with an undefined spleen get hijacked by fears that were never theirs: you soak up everyone around you's dread and crank it up — so the worry that jolts you awake at 3am is very possibly not even yours.

Real intuition is quiet and speaks once. Borrowed fear is loud, grinds on and on, and freezes you into doing nothing. Miss the difference and you'll spend a lifetime stuck in place by a dread you can't name.

Here's the twist: splenic fear only clears through action. The longer you stand there afraid, the bigger it gets — actually take the step, and you find, huh, that wasn't so bad after all.
en.editorNote: For the open-spleen friend who's often anxious but can't say what they're afraid of. The first question to ask: is this fear even mine?
variations: The open-spleen lesson: tell 'this is my intuition warning me' apart from 'this is just someone else's fear I absorbed and amplified.' The second one dissolves the moment you move.

items.sourceNote: internal only

zh-tw.content:
人類圖裡有個很殘忍但很好用的指標，叫「非自己主題」：當你活得不像自己，身體會用一種固定的情緒來抗議。

生產者是「挫折」，投射者是「苦澀」，顯示者是「憤怒」，反映者是「失望」。這些不是要你消滅的壞情緒，而是一盞警示燈——它亮，代表你剛剛又照著別人的規則、而不是自己的內在權威在做決定。

所以下次又莫名很煩的時候，先別急著怪環境。問問自己：這個決定，到底是我真心想要的，還是我以為我「應該」要的？
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合常常覺得「明明很努力卻不快樂」的人，用來自我檢查是不是活在別人的期待裡。
variations: 挫折、苦澀、憤怒、失望——這四種情緒不是你的敵人，是你偏離自己時，身體幫你點的警示燈。

en.content:
Human Design has a brutal but useful gauge called your "Not-Self theme": when you're living against your design, your body protests with one specific emotion.

Generators get frustration. Projectors get bitterness. Manifestors get anger. Reflectors get disappointment. These aren't bad feelings to stamp out — they're a warning light. When it flips on, it means you just made a decision by someone else's rules instead of your own inner authority.

So next time you're weirdly irritated, don't rush to blame your circumstances. Ask yourself: did I actually want this, or did I just think I was supposed to?
en.editorNote: For anyone who keeps thinking 'I'm working so hard, so why am I not happy?' A quick self-check for whether you're living inside someone else's expectations.
variations: Frustration, bitterness, anger, disappointment — these four aren't your enemies. They're the dashboard light your body flicks on when you've drifted off your own path.

items.sourceNote: internal only

zh-tw.content:
薦骨權威的人做決定，其實不是用「想」的，是用身體那聲「嗯哼」或「嗯嗯」。

「嗯哼」（往上揚）是身體在說要，「嗯嗯」（往下沉）是身體在說不要。這個聲音比你腦袋的長篇大論誠實太多了——腦袋會找一百個理由說服你答應，但薦骨從不解釋，它只是有力氣、或沒力氣。

重點是：薦骨只回應「當下被問到的事」，它不會主動幫你規劃人生。所以與其坐著空想「我到底要不要」，不如讓別人問你、或你大聲問自己，然後聽身體先出聲。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合純生產者朋友，教他們別再用頭腦硬想決定，而是信任身體最直接的那一聲反應。
variations: 薦骨不會說謊：有力氣接近的就是對的，一想到就累的就是不對的。它不解釋，它只是誠實。

en.content:
People with Sacral authority don't actually decide with their heads — they decide with a gut sound: an "uh-huh" or an "uh-uh."

The rising "uh-huh" is the body saying yes. The sinking "uh-uh" is the body saying no. That sound is far more honest than your brain's long-winded case — your mind will find a hundred reasons to say yes, but the Sacral never explains. It just has energy, or it doesn't.

The catch: the Sacral only responds to what's put in front of it right now. It won't plan your life for you. So instead of sitting there overthinking 'do I or don't I,' let someone ask you — or ask yourself out loud — and listen for the body's sound first.
en.editorNote: For pure Generators. Teaches them to stop grinding decisions out in their heads and trust the body's most immediate response.
variations: The Sacral doesn't lie: if you have energy to move toward it, it's a yes; if the thought alone tires you out, it's a no. It doesn't explain — it's just honest.

items.sourceNote: internal only

zh-tw.content:
情緒權威的人，人生只有一條鐵律：沒有當下的真相，只有時間過後才浮出來的清明。

你的情緒像海浪，會一路高、也會一路低。在浪頭上，什麼都想答應；在浪底，什麼都想拒絕——這兩個都不是真正的你，只是你在浪上的位置。真正的答案，要等浪平了、你既不特別興奮也不特別低落的時候，才會安靜浮現。

所以請對世界說：「我需要想一下、晚點回你。」這句話不是拖延，是你這輩子最重要的護身符。衝動答應的，往往是你隔天最後悔的。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合情緒中心有定義的人，提醒他們重大決定一定要睡過一晚、跨越情緒波再說。
variations: 情緒權威的口頭禪應該是：「給我一點時間。」浪頭上的興奮和浪底的沮喪都不算數，等浪平了再決定。

en.content:
If you have Emotional authority, your life runs on one iron rule: there is no truth in the now — clarity only surfaces once time has passed.

Your emotions move like a wave, riding high and then dipping low. At the crest you want to say yes to everything; at the trough you want to refuse everything — and neither is the real you, just where you happen to be on the wave. The true answer only rises quietly once the water flattens and you're neither especially excited nor especially down.

So say it to the world: "I need to sleep on it, I'll get back to you." That's not stalling — it's the most important safeguard of your life. The things you say yes to on impulse are usually the ones you regret by morning.
en.editorNote: For people with a defined emotional center. A reminder that big decisions must wait — sleep on it, ride out the wave first.
variations: The Emotional authority mantra should be: 'Give me some time.' The excitement at the crest and the gloom at the trough both don't count — decide once the wave settles.

items.sourceNote: internal only

zh-tw.content:
投射者的氣場是「聚焦又穿透」的：它會鎖定一個人，直直穿進對方的核心，一眼把人看到底。

所以你身邊那個投射者朋友，常常一句話就戳中你不想承認的地方——他不是愛管閒事，是氣場天生就這樣運作，看得又深又準。

但這份天賦有個前提：要有人先「邀請」他看，他才不會顯得咄咄逼人。沒被邀請就急著給建議，再準的話都會變成別人耳裡的雜音。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個「一針見血、卻常常好心被當驢肝肺」的投射者朋友，提醒他等對方問了再說。
variations: 投射者的眼睛像 X 光：一掃就看穿。差別只在——被邀請時是天賦，沒被邀請時是白目。

en.content:
A Projector's aura is focused and penetrating: it locks onto one person, cuts straight into their core, and reads them all the way down in a single glance.

So the Projector friend in your life keeps naming the exact thing you didn't want to admit — they're not being nosy, their aura just works like that: deep and scarily accurate.

But the gift comes with a condition: someone has to invite them to look, or it lands as pushy. Give advice before you're asked and even the most spot-on read turns into noise in the other person's ears.
en.editorNote: Send to the Projector friend who's dead-on but keeps getting their good intentions thrown back at them. The reminder: wait until they ask.
variations: A Projector's eyes are like an X-ray: one scan and they see right through you. The only difference — invited, it's a gift; uninvited, it's overstepping.

items.sourceNote: internal only

zh-tw.content:
頭腦中心有定義的人，腦袋像一台關不掉的引擎：不用外界刺激，自己就會源源不絕地冒問題、冒靈感。

好處是你天生有一股「想搞懂一切」的動力；壞處是同一股動力，半夜兩點還在逼你思考早就該放下的事。

重點是——你腦中那些問題，大多是「拿來想的」，不是「拿來馬上解決的」。學會讓念頭來、也讓它走，別每個都當成非答不可的作業，你會輕鬆很多。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「腦袋停不下來、常常想到失眠」的頭腦有定義朋友，提醒他念頭不等於待辦。
variations: 頭腦有定義的人：不是你想太多，是你的引擎本來就不會熄火。學會讓它空轉，別每個念頭都踩油門。

en.content:
If your Head center is defined, your mind is an engine you can't switch off: with no outside prompt at all, it keeps generating its own questions and flashes of inspiration.

The upside is a built-in drive to understand everything. The downside is that same drive is still making you chew on something at 2am that you should've dropped hours ago.

Here's the thing — most of those questions are for thinking about, not for solving right now. Learn to let a thought arrive and let it leave, instead of treating every one as homework due tonight, and life gets a lot lighter.
en.editorNote: For the defined-Head friend whose brain won't quiet down and thinks itself into insomnia. A reminder that a thought isn't a to-do.
variations: Defined Head center: it's not that you think too much — your engine just never idles down. Learn to let it rev in neutral instead of flooring it for every stray thought.

items.sourceNote: internal only

zh-tw.content:
全世界大概七成的人，頭腦中心是空白的——意思是你腦裡那些讓你焦慮到睡不著的問題，很多根本不是你的。

空白的頭腦像海綿，會把身邊每個人的疑惑、煩惱、待解決的事全吸進來，還放大成「我一定要想通」。難怪你常常為了一件跟你八竿子打不著的事，糾結一整晚。

下次腦袋又轉個不停時，先問一句：「這個問題，到底需不需要我來解決？」很多時候答案是不用——放下它，那不是你的功課。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「總在替別人的事操心、想到睡不著」的空白頭腦朋友，提醒他先分辨問題是誰的。
variations: 空白頭腦的功課：分清楚「這是我真的想懂的」跟「這只是我吸進來、別人的煩惱」——後者，放下就好。

en.content:
Around seventy percent of people have an undefined Head center — meaning a lot of the questions keeping you up at night were never yours to begin with.

An open Head is a sponge: it soaks up everyone around you's doubts, worries, and unsolved problems, then blows them up into 'I have to figure this out.' No wonder you spend a whole night tangled up in something that has nothing to do with you.

So next time your mind won't stop spinning, ask one question first: 'Does this actually need me to solve it?' More often than not the answer is no — let it go, it was never your assignment.
