en.editorNote: For the open-Head friend who's forever fretting over other people's problems and losing sleep. A reminder to first sort out whose problem it even is.
variations: The open-Head lesson: tell 'this is something I genuinely want to understand' apart from 'this is just someone else's worry I absorbed.' The second one — just put it down.

items.sourceNote: internal only

zh-tw.content:
反映者全身的能量中心都是空白的，氣場像在「試吃」周圍的一切：你會短暫嚐到別人的情緒、狀態，然後如實映照出來。

所以對反映者來說，「待在哪裡、跟誰在一起」比任何事都重要。同一個人，換到對的地方會整個發光，困在錯的環境會慢慢黯淡——不是你變了，是你如實反映了那個地方的品質。

如果你最近莫名低落，先別急著檢討自己。看看你花最多時間的那個空間、那群人——換個環境，你可能就像換了個人。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「換了地方就整個人不一樣」的反映者朋友，提醒他環境對他是頭等大事。
variations: 反映者不是情緒不穩，是你像一面鏡子，忠實照出你所在的地方好不好——想活得亮，先站對地方。

en.content:
A Reflector has every energy center undefined, so their aura is basically 'sampling' everything around them: you briefly taste other people's moods and states, then mirror them right back.

That's why, for a Reflector, where you are and who you're with matters more than anything else. The same person lights up completely in the right place and slowly dims in the wrong one — you didn't change, you just faithfully reflected the quality of that space.

So if you've felt inexplicably low lately, don't rush to blame yourself. Look at the room and the people you spend the most time with — change the environment, and you might feel like a whole new person.
en.editorNote: For the Reflector friend who becomes a different person the moment they change surroundings. A reminder that environment is their number-one priority.
variations: A Reflector isn't emotionally unstable — you're a mirror, faithfully showing whether the place you're in is healthy. Want to shine? Stand in the right spot first.

items.sourceNote: internal only

zh-tw.content:
反映者的重大決定，不是「想」出來的，是「等」跟「說」出來的：花一整個月亮週期，大概 28 天，把這件事反覆講給你信任的人聽。

這 28 天裡，月亮會輪流點亮你身上每個角落，讓你從各種不同的角度去感覺同一件事。你不是在拖，是在讓自己把整圈都走完——走完了，剩下來還站得住的那個答案，才是真的。

所以請找一兩個能安靜聽你說話的人，不是要他們給建議，是讓你聽見自己的聲音。答案不會從天上掉下來，是你講著講著，自己浮出來的。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合反映者朋友，提醒他重大決定別急著當場定案，用一個月、找人聊、聽自己說。
variations: 反映者做大決定的公式：一個月 + 一個願意聽你說話的人。答案不是想出來的，是說出來的。

en.content:
A Reflector doesn't think their big decisions through — they wait them out and talk them out: give it a full lunar cycle, about 28 days, and keep speaking the choice aloud to people you trust.

Over those 28 days the moon lights up every corner of you in turn, letting you feel the same decision from every angle. You're not stalling — you're letting yourself travel the whole loop. Whatever answer is still standing when the loop closes is the real one.

So find one or two people who'll just listen quietly — not to hand you advice, but so you can hear your own voice. The answer won't fall from the sky. It surfaces on its own, somewhere in the middle of you talking it out.
en.editorNote: For the Reflector friend — a reminder not to lock in a big decision on the spot, but to take a month, talk it through, and listen to themselves.
variations: A Reflector's formula for a big decision: one month + one person willing to listen. The answer isn't thought out — it's talked out.

items.sourceNote: internal only

zh-tw.content:
當一個「情緒有定義」的人，跟一個「情緒空白」的人在一起，會發生一件很微妙的事：那道情緒的浪，是有定義的人在放，空白的人整個接收下來，還放大兩倍。

所以吵架的時候，空白那方常常比正主還激動——不是他玻璃心，是他把對方的浪，錯認成自己的心情了。等對方浪一退、雨過天青，他卻還困在剛剛那波裡出不來。

如果你是空白那方，記得問自己一句：「這股情緒，本來是我的，還是我剛剛吸進來的？」分清楚了，你就不會替別人的浪，賠上自己一整晚。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合情侶或室友之間，一方情緒起伏大、一方莫名被帶著跑的組合，提醒空白方先分辨情緒的來源。
variations: 情緒空白的人跟情緒有定義的人相處，像沒帶傘站在別人的雨裡：淋濕的是你，天氣卻不是你的。

en.content:
When someone with a defined emotional center pairs up with someone whose emotional center is undefined, something subtle happens: the emotional wave belongs to the defined one, but the undefined one absorbs the whole thing — and cranks it up to double.

So in a fight, the undefined partner often ends up more worked up than the person the feeling actually came from. They're not overly sensitive — they mistook the other person's wave for their own mood. And once the defined partner's storm passes and the sky clears, the undefined one is still stuck in that swell, unable to climb out.

If you're the undefined one, ask yourself one thing: 'Was this feeling mine to begin with, or did I just breathe it in?' Once you can tell the difference, you stop paying for someone else's wave with your entire night.
en.editorNote: For couples or roommates where one runs emotionally intense and the other keeps getting swept along. A reminder for the undefined one to trace where the feeling actually started.
variations: An undefined emotional type with a defined one is like standing in someone else's rain with no umbrella: you're the one getting soaked, but the weather was never yours.

items.sourceNote: internal only

zh-tw.content:
薦骨空白的人，其實藏著一個天大的誤會：因為他短時間內可以爆發出比生產者還猛的能量，所以大家（包括他自己）都以為他很有電。

但關鍵字是「短時間」。空白的薦骨會把周圍的工作力、生命力全吸進來、還放大，讓你在衝刺期像上了發條——問題是這股電不是你自己發的，續航一到就直接沒電，而且連疲憊都會被放大成加倍的累。

所以薦骨空白的人，天生就不適合那種朝九晚五、日復一日的穩定消耗。你適合的是「衝一波、徹底休、再衝一波」。別拿自己去比別人的耐力——你的強項是爆發，不是續航。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「拚起來很猛、但撐不久、撐久就崩」的空白薦骨朋友，幫他理解自己不是懶，是設計不同。
variations: 薦骨空白不是沒力，是「借來的力」：短期能贏過任何人，長期硬撐一定燒光自己。衝完，記得躺平。

en.content:
People with an undefined Sacral center carry one huge misunderstanding: because they can burst out with more raw energy than a Generator for short stretches, everyone (themselves included) assumes they're an energizer bunny.

But the key word is 'short.' An open Sacral pulls in the work and life force of everyone around it and amplifies it, so during a sprint you feel wound up like a machine — the catch is, that charge was never yours to generate. When it runs out you flatline, and even the exhaustion gets amplified into double the tired.

So an undefined-Sacral person is simply not built for that steady, nine-to-five, day-after-day drain. What fits you is 'go hard, rest completely, go hard again.' Stop measuring yourself against other people's stamina — your strength is the burst, not the endurance.
en.editorNote: For the open-Sacral friend who can go hard but can't sustain it and crashes if they try. Helps them see they're not lazy — just built differently.
variations: An open Sacral isn't 'no energy' — it's 'borrowed energy': it can outrun anyone short-term, but grind long-term and it will burn itself out. After the sprint, lie flat.

items.sourceNote: internal only

zh-tw.content:
情緒中心空白的人，常把「一個人靜一靜」當成一種奢侈、甚至一種罪惡感——好像非得有正當理由才配獨處。

但對你來說，獨處不是奢侈品，是功能性的必需品。你一整天在外面吸滿了別人的情緒，沒有一段安靜的時間把這些倒掉，你根本分不清哪些心情是你自己的。那不是逃避社交，是幫自己「清機」。

所以下次想窩回房間、關掉群組、誰都不想理的時候，別再罪惡了。你不是冷漠，你只是需要把別人的情緒放下，才找得回原本的自己。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「社交完就想消失、又覺得對不起大家」的情緒空白朋友，告訴他獨處對他是必需品不是任性。
variations: 情緒空白的人需要獨處，不是想逃，是要清空——把白天吸進來的、別人的情緒倒掉，才找得回自己的心情。

en.content:
People with an undefined emotional center often treat 'some time alone' as a luxury — or even something to feel guilty about, as if they need a valid excuse to be by themselves.

But for you, alone time isn't a luxury. It's a functional necessity. You spend all day out there soaking up everyone's emotions, and without a stretch of quiet to pour them back out, you genuinely can't tell which moods are even yours. That's not avoiding people — that's clearing your cache.

So next time you want to retreat to your room, mute the group chat, and deal with no one, drop the guilt. You're not cold. You just need to set down other people's emotions before you can find your own self again.
en.editorNote: For the undefined-emotional friend who wants to disappear after socializing but feels bad about it. Tells them solitude is a necessity, not selfishness.
variations: An undefined emotional type needs alone time not to escape but to clear out — to pour back all the feelings they absorbed during the day before they can find their own mood again.

items.sourceNote: internal only

zh-tw.content:
情緒中心空白的人，在衝突裡有個最要命的盲點：你常常分不清，哪些情緒的邊界是你的，哪些是對方的。

對方一皺眉、一嘆氣，你就整個緊繃起來，急著想把氣氛救回來——結果對方其實只是累了，你卻已經替他難過、替他生氣、替他把整場戲演完了。這就是為什麼情緒空白的人特別容易變成「和事佬」，甚至是「討好型人格」：不是你愛討好，是你受不了那股不是你的低氣壓。

練習的第一步，是在心裡畫一條線：「這是他的情緒，我可以陪，但我不用扛。」你不負責讓每個人都開心——你只負責，別把別人的雨，錯穿成自己的衣服。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「一有人不開心就渾身不對勁、拼命想討好」的情緒空白朋友，幫他建立情緒界線。
variations: 情緒空白的人最該練的一句話：「這是你的情緒，不是我的。」你可以陪著，但你沒有義務替全世界的心情負責。

en.content:
In a conflict, people with an undefined emotional center have one fatal blind spot: you often can't tell where your feelings end and the other person's begin.

One frown, one sigh from them and your whole body tenses up, scrambling to rescue the mood — when in fact they're just tired, and you've already gotten sad for them, angry for them, and played out the entire scene on their behalf. That's exactly why undefined-emotional people so easily become the peacekeeper, even the people-pleaser: it's not that you love pleasing, it's that you can't stand a low-pressure front that isn't even yours.

Step one is drawing a line in your head: 'This is their emotion. I can be here for it, but I don't have to carry it.' You're not responsible for making everyone happy — you're only responsible for not mistaking someone else's rain for a coat of your own.
en.editorNote: For the undefined-emotional friend who feels wrong all over the second anyone's upset and rushes to please. Helps them build an emotional boundary.
variations: The line an undefined emotional type most needs to practice: 'This is your emotion, not mine.' You can stay by their side, but you're not obligated to answer for the whole world's moods.

items.sourceNote: Undefined throat — pressure to speak, over-talking to fill silence, chameleon voice that adapts to the room

zh-tw.content:
喉嚨中心空白的人，天生背著一股「一定要開口」的壓力：氣氛一安靜下來，你就手癢想講點什麼把空檔補起來。

所以你常常話一出口就後悔——不是你愛講，是那股「趕快說點話」的壓力在推你。其實你的聲音超有彈性，能配合不同的人切換語氣、講不同的內容，只是這份天賦要在「被問到、被點名」的時候才最漂亮。

練習閉嘴不是叫你冷場，是把力氣留給真正該你發聲的那一刻。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「一冷場就緊張、忍不住一直講」的喉嚨空白朋友，提醒他沉默沒關係。
variations: 喉嚨空白的功課：分清楚「我真的想說」跟「我只是受不了安靜」——後者講出來的話，通常事後最想收回。

en.content:
People with an undefined throat carry a built-in pressure to speak: the second things go quiet, your hands get itchy to say something and fill the gap.

That's why you so often regret words the moment they leave your mouth — it's not that you love talking, it's that 'quick, say something' pressure shoving you forward. In truth your voice is wildly flexible, able to shift tone and content to match whoever's in front of you — but that gift shines brightest when you're asked, when you're called on.

Learning to stay quiet isn't about killing the vibe. It's about saving your voice for the moment it's actually yours to speak.
en.editorNote: For the undefined-throat friend who panics at silence and can't stop talking. A reminder that quiet is allowed.
variations: The undefined throat's lesson: tell apart 'I actually want to say this' from 'I just can't stand the silence.' The second kind is what you'll want to take back later.

items.sourceNote: Defined vs undefined centers — 'is this mine or theirs?' the single most clarifying question in Human Design

zh-tw.content:
人類圖裡最能解開混亂的一句話，只有六個字：「這是我的，還是他的？」

你「有定義」的中心，是你穩定的原廠配備，走到哪都一樣；你「空白」的中心，則像海綿，會把周圍人的能量吸進來、還放大好幾倍。所以你在某些人身邊會突然變得特別焦慮、特別亢奮、特別沒安全感——那常常根本不是你，是你接收來的。

下次情緒莫名其妙飆起來，先別急著相信它，問一句：「這，是我的嗎？」很多時候答案是：不是。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「跟不同人相處就像變不同的人」的高敏感朋友，給他一個分辨情緒來源的問句。
variations: 你不是善變，你只是空白中心太誠實：走到哪，就把那個環境的能量老實地放大給你看。

en.content:
The single most clarifying question in Human Design is just five words: 'Is this mine or theirs?'

Your defined centers are your stable factory settings — the same wherever you go. Your undefined centers are sponges: they soak up the energy of the people around you and amplify it several times over. So around certain people you suddenly feel unusually anxious, hyped, or unsafe — and that often isn't you at all. It's what you picked up.

Next time a mood spikes out of nowhere, don't rush to believe it. Ask first: 'Is this even mine?' A lot of the time, the answer is no.
en.editorNote: For the highly-sensitive friend who becomes a different person with different people. A question to trace where a feeling actually came from.
variations: You're not fickle — your open centers are just brutally honest. Wherever you go, they faithfully amplify that room's energy right back at you.

items.sourceNote: G center / identity center — sense of self and direction; open G searches for identity and direction through others

zh-tw.content:
G 中心管的是兩件大事：你是誰、你要往哪走。

這裡「有定義」的人，自我感很穩，像內建指南針，方向感不太會晃；「空白」的人則相反，你的身分和方向會隨著環境流動——換個地方、換群人，你好像就變成另一種樣子。

所以空白 G 的人別急著逼自己「找到唯一的我」，你的設計本來就是流動的。與其硬定一個標籤，不如去對的地方、待在對的人身邊——對的環境，會自動把對的方向指給你看。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「一直在找自己是誰、卻越找越迷惘」的朋友，提醒他方向藏在環境裡。
variations: G 中心空白的人不是沒有自我，是你的自我會挑地方長：待錯地方會迷路，待對地方會發光。

en.content:
The G center runs two big things: who you are, and where you're headed.

People defined here have a steady sense of self — a built-in compass whose direction barely wavers. Open-G people are the opposite: your identity and direction flow with your surroundings. New place, new crowd, and you seem to become a different version of yourself.

So if your G is open, stop forcing yourself to 'find the one true me.' You're designed to be fluid. Instead of nailing down a label, go to the right places and stay near the right people — the right environment will point you toward the right direction all on its own.
en.editorNote: For the friend who keeps searching for who they are and only gets more lost. A reminder that direction hides in the environment.
variations: An open G center doesn't mean you have no self — it means your self is picky about where it grows: wrong place and you're lost, right place and you glow.

items.sourceNote: Generated channels / sacral — sustainable energy only for the right work; the 'turned on' gut yes vs mind-driven should

zh-tw.content:
薦骨的「回應」不是用想的，是身體對眼前這件事「亮起來」或「暗下去」的瞬間反應。

對的事，會讓你有一種被點亮、渾身通電的「想做」；不對的事，你會感覺整個人往後縮、能量提不起來。這個訊號不講道理、也不能談判——它比你腦袋列的「應該要做」清單準太多了。

所以撐得久的能量，只會給對的事。你不是沒毅力，你只是還沒遇到那個讓薦骨真心說「要」的東西。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「明明很努力卻總是半途而廢、懷疑自己沒毅力」的生產者朋友，幫他重新定義毅力。
variations: 生產者的毅力不是靠意志力硬撐，是靠遇到對的事——薦骨一亮，你自己都攔不住。

en.content:
The sacral 'response' isn't something you think out — it's your body lighting up or dimming down the instant something lands in front of you.

The right thing gives you a switched-on, fully-charged 'I want to do this.' The wrong thing makes your whole body pull back, energy refusing to rise. This signal doesn't argue and doesn't negotiate — and it's far more accurate than any 'should-do' list your head draws up.

So the energy that lasts only ever shows up for the right thing. You're not lacking willpower — you just haven't met the thing your sacral says a real 'yes' to yet.
en.editorNote: For the Generator friend who works hard, keeps quitting halfway, and doubts their own grit. Helps redefine what grit even is.
variations: A Generator's stamina isn't willpower gritted through — it's meeting the right thing. Once the sacral lights up, you couldn't stop yourself if you tried.

items.sourceNote: Defined throat — consistent reliable communication; the majority (72%) who can speak up on cue anywhere

zh-tw.content:
喉嚨中心「有定義」的人，是天生的表達穩定戶：不管場合大小、聽眾是誰，你都有一套一致又可靠的方式把話講出來。

開會要你臨時發言，你張口就來；別人還在腦內打草稿，你已經講完重點了。這是你的強項，但也藏一個小盲區——因為表達對你太容易，你有時會忘了，不是每個人都能像你這樣「想到就說得出來」。

所以在喉嚨空白的人面前，多留幾秒空檔，別急著替他把話補完。你的穩，正好可以撐住別人的猶豫。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「開會總是第一個發言、表達從不卡關」的喉嚨定義朋友，順便提醒他體諒慢熱的人。
variations: 喉嚨有定義的人不是比較敢講，是身體天生就有一條穩定的「輸出管線」——想法進去，話就出來。

en.content:
People with a defined throat are natural steady talkers: whatever the setting, whoever the audience, you've got a consistent, reliable way of getting the words out.

Called on to speak up mid-meeting? You just open your mouth and go. While others are still drafting in their heads, you've already made the point. It's a real strength — but it hides a small blind spot: because speaking comes so easily to you, you sometimes forget that not everyone can 'say it the moment they think it' the way you do.

So around undefined-throat people, leave a few extra beats of silence. Don't rush to finish their sentences. Your steadiness can hold space for someone else's hesitation.
en.editorNote: For the defined-throat friend who's always first to speak in meetings and never gets tongue-tied. Also a nudge to be patient with slower-to-warm-up folks.
variations: A defined throat doesn't make you bolder — your body just came with a stable output pipeline: thought goes in, words come out.

items.sourceNote: Single definition — ~41% of people; all defined centers form one connected circuit; energy is self-contained, processes internally, doesn't need others to feel whole

zh-tw.content:
「單一定義」的人，身上所有有定義的中心是連成一整條線路的——大約 41% 的人是這型。

意思是你的能量自成一個宇宙：一件事丟進來，你自己在心裡就能從頭想到尾，不太需要別人幫你接線。所以你常常一個人待著也很自在，甚至覺得「跟自己相處」比跟人相處還省電。

但這也有個小盲點：因為你不太需要外界輸入，有時會忘了別人是需要「一起想」才有辦法下結論的。別把自己的自給自足，當成全世界的標準。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「一個人就能把事情想通、不太依賴討論」的朋友，順便提醒他體諒需要外部激盪的人。
variations: 單一定義的人像一顆完整的電池，不用外接就能運轉——這不是孤僻，是原廠自帶的完整。

en.content:
If you have a Single Definition, all your defined centers link up into one continuous circuit — about 41% of people are wired this way.

It means your energy is a self-contained universe: drop a problem in and you can run it start to finish inside your own head, no external wiring required. That's why being alone feels easy for you — sometimes 'time with yourself' actually costs less battery than time with people.

The small blind spot: because you don't need much outside input, you can forget that other people have to 'think out loud, together' before they reach a conclusion. Don't treat your own self-sufficiency as the standard for everyone.
en.editorNote: For the friend who works things out solo and rarely needs to talk it through. Also a nudge to be patient with people who need to bounce ideas off others.
variations: A Single Definition is like a fully-charged battery that runs with nothing plugged in — that's not being antisocial, it's factory-installed completeness.

items.sourceNote: Split definition — ~46% of people; two separate circuits of defined centers not internally connected; unconsciously drawn to people/energy that bridge the gap; takes a little longer to decide

zh-tw.content:
「分裂定義」的人，身上有兩塊各自獨立的能量線路，中間沒有直接連起來——大約 46% 的人是這型，其實是最多數。

所以你有時會覺得自己內在像有兩個系統，各講各話，要花點時間才能「對上線」，做決定也因此比別人慢半拍。這不是你優柔寡斷，是你天生就需要一點時間，讓兩邊接上。

有趣的是，你會不自覺被某些人吸引——那些人身上剛好帶著你缺的那一段能量，一靠近，你分開的兩塊就被接起來了，整個人瞬間「完整」。所以別急著把那份心動全歸功於愛情，有時候只是你的線路，終於被接通了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「跟某些人相處就莫名很順、很完整」的朋友，幫他理解那份吸引力背後的能量邏輯。
variations: 分裂定義的人不是三心二意，是內在有兩塊拼圖，得等對的人、對的時間，才拼得起來。

en.content:
If you have a Split Definition, you carry two separate circuits of defined energy with no direct link between them — about 46% of people are built this way, actually the biggest group of all.

So sometimes you feel like two internal systems talking past each other, and it takes a beat for them to 'sync up.' That's also why decisions come a touch slower for you. It's not indecision — you're simply wired to need a moment for both sides to connect.

Here's the interesting part: you'll find yourself pulled toward certain people without knowing why — they happen to carry the exact energy you're missing, and the second they're near, your two halves bridge and you suddenly feel whole. So don't chalk every spark up to romance. Sometimes it's just your circuit finally getting completed.
en.editorNote: For the friend who feels inexplicably 'complete' and in flow around specific people. Helps them see the energetic logic behind that pull.
variations: A Split Definition isn't wishy-washy — you've got two puzzle pieces inside you that only click together with the right person, at the right time.

items.sourceNote: PHS / Determination — Human Design doesn't prescribe WHAT to eat but the CONDITIONS (manner, timing, focus) under which you best digest food and information

zh-tw.content:
人類圖有一套很少人知道的「飲食設計」，重點很反直覺：它不告訴你「該吃什麼」，只告訴你「該怎麼吃」。

有些人天生要專心吃、一次只做一件事，邊吃邊滑手機就消化不良；有些人反而要有點聲音、有人聊天才吃得下。同一盤菜，換個吃法，你身體吸收到的完全不一樣。

所以下次覺得「明明吃得很健康卻沒精神」，先別急著換食譜。問問自己：我是不是用了不適合我的方式在吃？消化的不只是食物，還有你每天接收的所有資訊。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「吃得很養生卻總是沒能量」的朋友，提醒他吃的方式可能比吃的內容更關鍵。
variations: 人類圖的飲食觀：重點不是盤子裡放什麼，是你用什麼狀態把它吃下去。

en.content:
Human Design has a little-known take on eating, and it's refreshingly counterintuitive: it doesn't tell you what to eat — only how to eat it.

Some people are built to eat in full focus, one thing at a time; scroll your phone mid-meal and digestion falls apart. Others actually need a bit of noise, some conversation, to eat well at all. Same plate of food, different way of eating it, and what your body absorbs is completely different.

So next time you think 'I eat so clean but I've got no energy,' don't rush to swap the recipe. Ask yourself: am I eating in a way that doesn't suit me? And it's not just food you digest — it's every bit of information you take in each day.
en.editorNote: For the friend who eats impeccably clean yet always feels low on energy. A reminder that how you eat may matter more than what you eat.
variations: Human Design's food philosophy: it's not about what's on the plate — it's the state you're in when you eat it.

items.sourceNote: PHS Environment variable — the right physical setting (light, sound, temperature, indoor/outdoor) is where a person's health and clarity are optimized; where you are shapes how you function

zh-tw.content:
人類圖裡有個常被忽略的變數，叫「環境」：它說的不是抽象的氛圍，是很具體的——你在什麼樣的光線、聲音、溫度、室內或戶外的空間，身體才會真正舒展開來。

有人一定要靠窗、有自然光才寫得出東西；有人要躲進沒窗的角落才靜得下心。這不是龜毛，是你的身體對「對的地方」很誠實。待錯環境，再自律都事倍功半；待對環境，狀態自己就上來了。

所以與其一直逼自己「更專注」，不如先問：我現在是不是坐在錯的位置上？換張桌子、換個角落，有時候比灌三杯咖啡還有用。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「在家怎樣都做不了事、換到咖啡廳就通了」的朋友，提醒他環境是生產力的隱藏開關。
variations: 你不是不夠自律，你只是還沒找到那個一坐下就對了的位置。

en.content:
Human Design has an often-overlooked variable called Environment — and it doesn't mean some abstract vibe. It's concrete: the kind of light, sound, temperature, indoor or outdoor space where your body actually unclenches and works.

Some people can only write facing a window with natural light; others need to tuck into a windowless corner before their mind will settle. That's not being fussy — your body is just honest about the right place. In the wrong setting, all the discipline in the world gets you half the result. In the right one, your focus rises on its own.

So instead of forcing yourself to 'concentrate harder,' ask first: am I sitting in the wrong spot? Sometimes changing tables or corners beats three cups of coffee.
en.editorNote: For the friend who can't get anything done at home but flows the moment they hit a cafe. A reminder that environment is a hidden productivity switch.
variations: You're not lacking discipline — you just haven't found the spot where everything clicks the moment you sit down.

items.sourceNote: Triple split definition — ~11% of people; three separate circuits activated by different people and settings; benefits from variety of input, meets and connects with people more slowly, needs busier environments

zh-tw.content:
「三重分裂定義」的人，身上有三塊各自獨立的能量線路——大約只有 11% 的人是這型，算是稀有款。

因為要接的線比較多，你認識人、對事情下判斷，都會比別人慢一點，需要不同的人、不同的場合，來輪流點亮你身上不同的部分。所以你反而不太適合「只跟同一個人、待在同一個地方」，太單一的環境會讓你有一塊始終沒被打開。

這也是為什麼你常常在熱鬧、人來人往的地方反而更自在——不是你愛湊熱鬧，是多元的刺激剛好餵飽了你那三條線。別逼自己安靜坐定，你的完整，是被各式各樣的人事物一起拼起來的。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「慢熟、但在多元環境裡特別活躍」的朋友，幫他理解為什麼單調的生活會讓他悶。
variations: 三重分裂的人不是難搞，是需要多一點人、多一點場景，才能把自己完整點亮。

en.content:
If you have a Triple Split Definition, you carry three separate circuits of energy — only about 11% of people are wired this way, so it's on the rare side.

Because there's more to connect, you meet people and reach conclusions a little slower than most, and you need different people and different settings to light up your different parts in turn. Which means you're not really built for 'one person, one place forever' — too monotone an environment leaves some part of you permanently switched off.

That's also why you often feel more at ease in busy, high-traffic places — it's not that you crave the crowd, it's that varied input is exactly what feeds those three circuits. Don't force yourself to sit still and quiet. Your wholeness gets assembled by a whole variety of people and things, together.
en.editorNote: For the slow-to-warm friend who comes alive in varied, bustling settings. Helps them see why a monotonous routine leaves them flat.
variations: A Triple Split isn't high-maintenance — you just need a few more people and a few more scenes to light yourself all the way up.

items.sourceNote: Ego / Heart authority — one of the rarest (1-2%); decisions run on willpower and genuine want, not emotion or gut

zh-tw.content:
「意志力（心）型權威」超級稀有，全世界只有 1~2% 的人是這型。他們做決定不靠情緒、也不靠直覺，就靠一句最誠實的問句：「我到底想不想？我有沒有那個心去做？」

如果心裡沒有那股「我要」，再合理、再該做的事，你也撐不了多久。你答應得出來，不代表你做得下去——重點永遠是你打從心底想不想。

所以別再用「應該」逼自己點頭了。問問你的心：這件事，我真的有意願承諾嗎？沒有，就別勉強。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「一時心軟答應一堆事、最後全爆掉」的朋友，提醒他先確認自己是不是真心想做。
variations: 心型權威的決策公式只有一條：我想不想。想，就全力衝；不想，別硬撐。

en.content:
Ego (Heart) Authority is seriously rare — only 1 to 2% of people have it. These folks don't decide by emotion or gut. They decide on one brutally honest question: 'Do I actually want this? Do I have the heart to see it through?'

If that 'I want to' isn't there, it doesn't matter how reasonable or how 'should' the thing is — you won't last. Being able to say yes isn't the same as being able to follow through. It always comes down to whether you truly want it.

So stop 'should'-ing yourself into things. Ask your heart: do I genuinely have the will to commit here? If not, don't force the nod.
en.editorNote: For the friend who caves and says yes to everything, then flames out. A reminder to check whether they actually want it first.
variations: A Heart type's decision formula is one line: do I want to? If yes, go all in. If no, don't force it.

items.sourceNote: Manifesting Generator conditioning — told to slow down, pick one thing, explain themselves; the 'too much / all over the place' wound reframed

zh-tw.content:
顯示生產者最常被念的一句話就是：「你能不能專心把一件事做完再說？」從小被要求慢下來、選一條路、把話講清楚才准動作——被念久了，真的會開始懷疑自己是不是「太亂、太貪心、什麼都做不完」。

但你不是不專一，你是天生要邊做邊修、同時開好幾條線才有電。硬把你塞進「一次只能一件事」的框，才是真的在耗你。

所以別再為「我怎麼又沒做完」自責了。你的完成，本來就長得跟別人不一樣。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種從小被說「三分鐘熱度、做事沒定性」的顯示生產者，幫他從那份罪惡感裡鬆綁。
variations: 顯示生產者不是做不完，是別人用「照順序做完一件」的尺，在量一台本來就要多工的引擎。

en.content:
The lecture a Manifesting Generator hears most: 'Can you please just finish one thing before starting another?' Told from childhood to slow down, pick one lane, spell it all out before acting — hear it enough and you really do start wondering if you're 'too scattered, too greedy, incapable of finishing anything.'

But you're not unfocused. You're wired to build and edit on the fly, several lanes open at once to stay charged. Cramming you into 'one thing at a time' is what actually drains you.

So stop beating yourself up over 'why didn't I finish again.' Your version of done was always going to look different.
en.editorNote: For the Manifesting Generator told since childhood they 'have no follow-through.' Help them loosen the guilt.
variations: A Manifesting Generator isn't failing to finish — people are just measuring a built-for-multitasking engine with a 'one-thing-in-order' ruler.

items.sourceNote: Open / undefined G Center (~40%) — no fixed identity or direction; gift of sampling many roles; direction surfaces via the right environment, not by thinking

zh-tw.content:
開放（未定義）G 中心的人，大概佔了四成，最常有的困惑是：「我到底是誰？我這輩子要往哪去？」你今天很篤定、明天又整個變一個人，總覺得別人都有明確的方向，只有你飄來飄去。

但這不是你有問題，這正是你的天賦：你能自然地變換角色、走進不同的人生，不被單一的「我就是這種人」綁死。你的方向不是用「想」出來的，是走進「對的環境、對的人身邊」時，自己浮現的。

所以別急著逼自己「定下來、想清楚我是誰」。先找到讓你舒服的地方，答案會自己來找你。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「常覺得自己沒方向、很羨慕別人目標明確」的朋友，提醒他方向是被環境帶出來的，不是硬想出來的。
variations: 開放 G 中心的人不是迷路，是你的路本來就不用先畫好——走對地方，路就會自己亮起來。

en.content:
About 40% of people have an open (undefined) G Center, and their most common ache is: 'Who even am I? Where is my life supposed to go?' Rock-solid today, a totally different person tomorrow — always feeling like everyone else has a fixed direction and you're the only one drifting.

But nothing's wrong with you — this is the gift: you can naturally shift roles and step into different lives without being locked into one 'this is just who I am.' Your direction isn't something you think your way to; it surfaces when you're in the right place, around the right people.

So stop forcing yourself to 'settle down and figure out who I am.' Find the spot where you feel at home first, and the answer will come find you.
en.editorNote: For the friend who feels directionless and envies people with clear goals. A reminder that direction is drawn out by environment, not thought up.
variations: An open G Center isn't lost — your path was never meant to be drawn in advance. Stand in the right place and it lights up on its own.

items.sourceNote: Open Throat Center — not-self theme of 'trying to get attention'; over-talking to be seen; power is in waiting to be asked

zh-tw.content:
開放喉嚨中心的人，有個很藏不住的小毛病：一緊張、一想被看見，就會忍不住多講話、搶著開口，講完又懊惱「我剛剛幹嘛講那麼多」。

那個「一直想引起注意」的衝動，其實不是你真的話多，是開放的喉嚨在替你放大「好想被聽見」的焦慮。你不必用音量證明自己的存在。

真正的力量，是等到對的時機、被問到的時候再開口——那一句話，會比你搶著講的十句都有份量。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「開會講太多、事後又超後悔」的朋友，提醒他不用急著填滿每一個空檔。
variations: 開放喉嚨的人不是話多，是太怕被忽略。學會閉嘴等一下，你的話反而變更有人聽。

en.content:
People with an open Throat Center have a tell they can't quite hide: the second they get nervous or want to be noticed, they can't help talking more, jumping in to speak — then cringe afterward, 'why did I say all that?'

That urge to keep grabbing attention isn't you being genuinely chatty. It's your open throat amplifying the anxiety of 'I need to be heard.' You don't have to prove you exist with volume.

Real power is waiting for the right moment — speaking when you're actually asked. That one line will carry more weight than the ten you rushed to say.
en.editorNote: For the friend who talks too much in meetings and regrets it every time. A reminder they don't have to fill every silence.
variations: An open Throat isn't too talkative — it's too afraid of being overlooked. Learn to hush and wait, and people actually start listening.

items.sourceNote: Open / undefined Head Center — mental pressure from other people's questions; built to ponder for fun, not to solve everyone's problems

zh-tw.content:
開放（未定義）頭腦中心的人，很容易被別人的問題「綁架」：明明不關你的事，你卻整晚睡不著，幫全世界想「這該怎麼辦、那該怎麼辦」。

那股「一定要想出答案」的壓力，很多根本不是你的，是你從環境裡吸進來、又放大的。頭腦中心天生是拿來「想好玩的事」，不是拿來「解決每個人的煩惱」。

所以下次腦袋停不下來時，先問一句：這個問題，到底是不是我需要回答的？很多時候，答案是「不是」，那你就可以放下了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「很愛替別人操心、腦袋停不下來」的朋友，提醒他不是每個問題都需要他來解。
variations: 開放頭腦的人不是想太多，是把整個世界的問號都撿回家扛。放下不是你的那些，你會輕鬆一半。

en.content:
People with an open (undefined) Head Center get easily hijacked by other people's questions: it's none of your business, yet you're up all night solving 'what should they do about this, what about that' for the entire world.

That pressure to 'come up with an answer' is often not even yours — you absorbed it from your environment and then amplified it. The Head Center was built to think about things that are fun to ponder, not to fix everyone's problems.

So next time your mind won't stop, ask one thing: is this question actually mine to answer? A lot of the time the answer is no — and then you get to put it down.
en.editorNote: For the friend who worries about everyone else and can't switch their brain off. A reminder that not every question is theirs to solve.
variations: An open Head Center isn't overthinking — it's carrying home every question mark in the world. Drop the ones that aren't yours and you're half as light.

items.sourceNote: Saturn Return (~age 28-30) x Human Design — the cosmic shakeup that forces you off the borrowed path back to your own design

zh-tw.content:
大概 28 到 30 歲那陣子，很多人會突然覺得「我現在過的到底是不是我要的人生」——工作想離職、感情想重來、整個人像被系統重灌。

那不是你走霉運，是「土星回歸」剛好來敲門，配上人類圖看更清楚：它在幫你把那些「別人幫你選的路」一條條抖掉。

所以那段亂糟糟的時期別怕，它不是在拆你，是在逼你長回自己原本的樣子。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合傳給那個快 30、突然什麼都想砍掉重練的朋友，告訴他這是轉大人的正常班表。
variations: 28 歲那場「人生大地震」不是懲罰，是宇宙在幫你把不適合的東西搖下來。

en.content:
Somewhere around 28 to 30, a lot of people get hit with a gut-punch of 'wait, is this life even the one I wanted?' — you want to quit the job, redo the relationship, factory-reset the whole self.

That's not a run of bad luck. It's your Saturn Return knocking, and through a Human Design lens it's even clearer: it's shaking loose, one by one, all the paths other people picked for you.

So don't fear the messy stretch. It isn't tearing you down — it's forcing you to grow back into who you actually are.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend nearing 30 who suddenly wants to burn it all down and start over. Tell them this is just the standard coming-of-age schedule.
variations: That 'life earthquake' at 28 isn't a punishment — it's the universe shaking off everything that was never yours to carry.

items.sourceNote: Open / undefined Solar Plexus (emotional) center — absorbs and amplifies others' moods; not-self theme of avoiding confrontation and people-pleasing

zh-tw.content:
情緒中心開放（未定義）的人，超怕氣氛尷尬：只要旁邊有人心情不好，你就整顆心跟著揪起來，還會忍不住想「是不是我害的、我要不要哄一下」。

那些不安其實有一半根本不是你的，是你把別人的情緒接進來又放大了。所以你才會為了不吵架，什麼都先說「好」，最後把自己憋到內傷。

練習分清楚：這份難受是我的，還是我接收來的？很多時候你只要不接，情緒就過了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「別人一臭臉就緊張、拚命想討好」的朋友，提醒他不是所有情緒都要他負責。
variations: 情緒中心開放的人不是玻璃心，是把整個房間的情緒都接進身體裡。

en.content:
People with an open (undefined) Solar Plexus dread an awkward vibe: the second someone nearby is in a bad mood, your whole chest tightens and you can't stop wondering 'did I cause this? should I go smooth it over?'

Half that unease isn't even yours — you pulled in someone else's emotion and cranked up the volume on it. That's why you say 'sure, fine' to everything just to dodge a fight, and quietly bottle yourself into knots.

Practice telling them apart: is this feeling mine, or did I just catch it? A lot of the time, if you don't pick it up, it simply passes.
en.editorNote: For the friend who panics the moment someone frowns and scrambles to please. A reminder that not every emotion is theirs to fix.
variations: An open Solar Plexus isn't thin-skinned — it just takes the whole room's mood in through the body.

items.sourceNote: Open / undefined Root center — adrenaline pressure to hurry and be free of the to-do list; rushing under borrowed stress

zh-tw.content:
根中心開放的人，心裡總有個聲音在催：「快點做完、快點清空，做完我就自由了。」結果你越急越亂，事情做完一件又冒出三件，那股壓力永遠清不完。

關鍵在於——那份「非得趕快」的急，很多根本不是你自己的，是你從環境裡吸來又放大的。你把別人的緊迫感當成自己的截止日在跑。

下次心慌想衝的時候先停三秒問：這件事真的急，還是我又接收到別人的焦慮了？分清楚，你就不用一直被推著跑。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「永遠在趕、清空待辦才敢喘口氣」的朋友，提醒他那股急很可能不是他的。
variations: 根中心開放的人不是沒耐性，是把全世界的「快一點」都吸進身體，還當成自己的死線。

en.content:
People with an open Root have a voice constantly nagging: 'hurry up, clear the list, once it's done I'll finally be free.' But the more you rush, the messier it gets — finish one thing, three more appear, and the pressure never actually clears.

Here's the catch: that 'I have to hurry' urgency often isn't even yours. You absorbed it from your environment and amplified it, running other people's rush like it's your own deadline.

Next time the panic to sprint hits, pause three seconds and ask: is this actually urgent, or did I just catch someone else's anxiety again? Tell them apart and you stop getting shoved through life.
en.editorNote: For the friend who's always rushing and only breathes once the to-do list is empty. A reminder that the pressure probably isn't theirs.
variations: An open Root isn't impatient — it inhales the whole world's 'hurry up' and treats it like a personal deadline.

items.sourceNote: Electromagnetic connection — two people each carry half a channel; together they complete it, explaining instant magnetic chemistry

zh-tw.content:
人類圖裡有種浪漫叫「電磁連結」：你身上有半條通道，剛好對方有另外半條，兩個人湊在一起，那條通道才會完整亮起來。

這就是為什麼有些人一見面就莫名來電、有種「被補齊」的感覺——不是幻覺，是你們的能量真的接上了。

當然，能互補的地方也常常是最容易吵的地方。他有的正是你缺的，那份吸引力跟摩擦力，本來就是同一條線。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合傳給那種「跟某個人就是特別有火花、又特別容易鬥嘴」的朋友，幫他解釋那股拉扯。
variations: 會讓你一見鍾情的人，常常握著你缺的那半條通道——難怪又吸引又想掐死對方。

en.content:
Human Design has a romance to it called the electromagnetic connection: you carry half a channel, they happen to carry the other half, and only when you're together does that channel light up whole.

That's why some people spark the instant you meet — that 'I feel completed' pull. It's not your imagination; your energies literally clicked into place.

Of course, the spot where you complete each other is often the exact spot you clash. What they have is what you lack — attraction and friction were always the same wire.
en.editorNote: For the friend who has undeniable chemistry with someone and also bickers with them nonstop. Explain the tug-of-war for them.
variations: The person you fall for at first sight is often holding the half-channel you're missing — no wonder you're drawn to them and want to strangle them.

items.sourceNote: Open / undefined Sacral (Projectors, Manifestors, Reflectors) — no fixed life-force; not-self theme of not knowing when enough is enough; overworking to keep up with Generators

zh-tw.content:
薦骨中心開放的人（投射者、顯示者、反映者），沒有生產者那種源源不絕的電，最容易掉進一個坑：不知道什麼時候該停。

因為你借來了別人的薦骨能量，一時間覺得「我還行、再撐一下」，等到發現不行，往往已經榨乾好幾天。你不是不夠拚，你是根本沒有「累了會自動喊停」的內建開關。

所以你要主動幫自己畫線：時間到就收工，不用跟生產者比誰坐得久。累之前先停，才是你的省電模式。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「明明沒那個體力、卻硬要跟大家拚到底」的投射者或顯示者朋友，提醒他適可而止不是偷懶。
variations: 薦骨開放的人不是懶，是缺一個「夠了」的煞車——沒人幫你踩，你就會一路衝到燒掉。

en.content:
People with an open Sacral (Projectors, Manifestors, Reflectors) don't have a Generator's endless battery, and they fall into one classic trap: not knowing when to stop.

Because you've borrowed someone else's Sacral energy, for a while it feels like 'I'm fine, just a bit longer' — and by the time you realize you're not, you've usually run on empty for days. You're not lazy; you just don't come with the built-in 'tired means stop' switch Generators have.

So you have to draw the line yourself: when time's up, clock out — no competing with Generators over who sits longer. Stopping before you crash is your version of power-saving mode.
en.editorNote: For the Projector or Manifestor friend who doesn't have the stamina but insists on grinding with everyone else. A reminder that knowing when to stop isn't slacking.
variations: An open Sacral isn't lazy — it's missing an 'enough' brake. If no one hits it for you, you'll floor it straight into burnout.

items.sourceNote: Undefined Head Center — amplifies others' mental pressure; chasing answers to questions that aren't yours

zh-tw.content:
頭腦中心開放的人，最容易替全世界煩惱：別人的問題、別人的疑惑，一股腦全接進自己腦袋，然後熬夜幫他們想答案。

重點是，那些問題根本不是你的。你以為「想不通就代表我有問題」，其實只是接收了太多不屬於你的思緒。

學會分辨：這個問題是我真的想懂，還是我只是被別人的焦慮傳染了？分清楚，腦袋就安靜一半。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那種「別人一句話就能讓他失眠一整晚」的朋友，提醒他不是每個問題都需要他來解。
variations: 頭腦開放的人不是愛想太多，是把整個房間的問號都吸進來了。
不是每個冒出來的問題都要你回答——有些根本是別人掉在你腦袋裡的。

en.content:
People with an open Head Center end up worrying on behalf of the whole world: other people's problems, other people's questions — all of it pours straight into your head, and then you're up till 3am solving it for them.

Here's the thing: those questions were never yours. You assume 'if I can't figure it out, something's wrong with me,' when really you just absorbed a pile of thoughts that don't belong to you.

Learn to sort it: is this a question I actually want answered, or did I just catch someone else's anxiety? Sort that out and half the noise goes quiet.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who can be kept awake all night by one offhand comment. A reminder that not every question is theirs to solve.
variations: An open Head isn't 'overthinking' — it's vacuuming up every question mark in the room.
Not every question that pops up is yours to answer. Some were just dropped into your head by someone else.

items.sourceNote: Defined Head Center — a steady inner idea factory; constant inspiration and questions that won't switch off

zh-tw.content:
頭腦中心有定義的人，腦袋是一座 24 小時不打烊的靈感工廠：睡前一個念頭、洗澡一個念頭、開會開到一半又冒三個。

好消息是你從不缺點子，壞消息是你也關不掉。半夜三點還在跟自己開會，隔天頂著黑眼圈說「我昨天想通一件事」。

那些靈感是你的天賦，但別急著把每一個都變成待辦事項——有些想法只是路過，不是要你立刻執行。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那種「靈感多到睡不著、筆記本寫滿卻做不完」的創意型朋友，幫他跟自己的腦袋和解。
variations: 頭腦有定義的人沒有「放空」這個功能，只有「換一個念頭想」。
你的腦袋不是停不下來，是本來就設計成一直在運轉的——別怪它，餵它對的東西就好。

en.content:
If your Head Center is defined, your mind is a 24-hour idea factory that never closes: one thought at bedtime, one in the shower, three more halfway through a meeting.

Good news, you never run out of ideas. Bad news, you can't switch it off either. Still holding meetings with yourself at 3am, then showing up with eye bags going 'I figured something out last night.'

Those sparks are your gift — just don't rush to turn every one into a to-do. Some thoughts are only passing through; they're not asking to be executed right now.
en.editorNote: For the creative friend with too many ideas to sleep and a notebook full of things they'll never finish. Help them make peace with their own head.
variations: A defined Head Center doesn't come with a 'blank mind' setting — only a 'think about something else' one.
Your mind isn't refusing to stop — it was built to keep running. Don't fight it; just feed it the right things.

items.sourceNote: Channels — two gates connecting two centers create definition; explains your most consistent, repeatable energy patterns

zh-tw.content:
如果說「類型」是你的作業系統，那「通道」就是你那些改都改不掉的習慣。

每條通道由兩端接起來，接通了，那股能量就會固定在你身上跑一輩子——難怪有些行為你從小到大都一樣，不是你固執，是那條線一直亮著。

所以與其逼自己「改掉」那些特質，不如問：這條天生就亮的線，怎麼用在對的地方？
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合傳給那種「知道自己某個毛病、卻怎麼都改不掉」的朋友，換個角度看：那可能是天賦，不是缺點。
variations: 你那些「從小到大都這樣」的地方，很可能不是壞習慣，是一條一直亮著的通道。
通道就是你身上那條「永遠會這樣反應」的線——認識它，比對抗它省力多了。

en.content:
If your Type is the operating system, your Channels are the habits you can never quite quit.

Each channel is two ends wired together, and once it's lit, that energy runs through you for life — which is why some behaviors have been the same since you were a kid. You're not stubborn; that particular wire has just always been on.

So instead of forcing yourself to 'fix' those traits, ask: how do I aim this wire — the one that's lit by design — at the right thing?
en.editorNote: For the friend who knows one of their 'flaws' and can't shake it no matter what. A reframe: it might be a gift, not a defect.
variations: The stuff that's 'been like this your whole life' probably isn't a bad habit — it's a channel that's always been switched on.
A channel is the wire in you that 'always reacts this way.' Getting to know it beats fighting it every time.

items.sourceNote: Undefined Throat Center — pressure to be heard; over-talking or forcing attention as the not-self pattern

zh-tw.content:
喉嚨中心開放的人，常常會有一種「不講點什麼就會被忽略」的壓力，於是在飯桌上一直找話講、硬要插一句，講完又覺得「我剛剛幹嘛講那個」。

其實你不需要靠搶話證明你在場。真正屬於你的發言，會在對的時機自己冒出來，而且一開口就有份量。

練習一件事：不確定要不要講的時候，先閉嘴等一下。該你說的，宇宙會幫你遞麥克風。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那種「聚會裡拚命找話題、回家又懊惱自己太多嘴」的朋友，提醒他安靜也是一種存在感。
variations: 喉嚨開放的人不是話多，是被一股「快說點什麼」的壓力推著跑。
你不用搶著發言來證明你在——真正重要的話，會等到對的時機自己出場。

en.content:
People with an open Throat Center often feel a nagging pressure that 'if I don't say something, I'll be overlooked.' So they scramble for things to say at dinner, force a comment in — and then think 'why did I even say that?'

But you don't have to grab the floor to prove you're in the room. The words that are truly yours will surface at the right moment, and they'll land with weight the second you open your mouth.

Try this: when you're not sure whether to speak, zip it and wait a beat. What's yours to say, the universe will hand you the mic for.
en.editorNote: For the friend who works overtime to keep conversation going, then kicks themselves for talking too much on the way home. Silence is a presence too.
variations: An open Throat isn't 'chatty' — it's being pushed around by a 'say something, quick' pressure.
You don't have to grab the mic to prove you're here. The words that matter will show up right on time.

items.sourceNote: Undefined Will / Ego Center — over-promising and over-proving worth as the not-self theme; no consistent willpower to draw on

zh-tw.content:
意志力中心開放的人，最容易掉進「我要證明我值得」的迴圈：答應太多、扛太重、答應完才發現自己根本沒那個意志力硬撐。

因為你的意志力不是恆定供應的，你不能像有定義的人那樣「說到做到、咬牙撐完」——硬逼自己反而會累到懷疑人生。

你的價值從來不需要靠一次次逞強來換。少答應一點、少證明一點，你還是一樣有價值。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那種「明明累爆還是不敢說不、拚命想證明自己」的朋友，提醒他不用靠硬撐換認可。
variations: 意志力開放的人不是沒毅力，是那股力氣時有時無，不該拿來跟自己硬拚。
你不需要每次都證明自己值得——你本來就值得，不用靠逞強加碼。

en.content:
People with an open Will (Ego) Center fall hardest into the 'I have to prove I'm worth it' loop: promising too much, carrying too much — and only realizing afterward they don't actually have the steady willpower to muscle through it.

Your willpower isn't on constant supply. You can't do the 'say it, mean it, grit your teeth and finish' thing the way defined people can — force it and you'll grind yourself down to questioning your whole life.

Your worth was never something you had to earn by white-knuckling it again and again. Promise a little less, prove a little less — you're worth exactly the same.
en.editorNote: For the friend who's running on empty but still can't say no, endlessly trying to prove themselves. A reminder that worth isn't bought with sheer grit.
variations: An open Will isn't 'no willpower' — it's willpower that comes and goes, and shouldn't be spent grinding against yourself.
You don't have to prove you're worth it every single time. You already are — no white-knuckling required.

items.sourceNote: Incarnation Cross — life purpose from the conscious/unconscious Sun and Earth gates; ~70% of the imprint; embodies gradually after Saturn Return

zh-tw.content:
類型講的是你「怎麼做事」，人生角色講你「怎麼被看見」，而人生使命（Incarnation Cross）講的是你「來這一趟到底要幹嘛」。

它由四個閘門組成，卻佔了你整張圖大約七成的份量——換句話說，這是你這輩子最大的主題曲。

只是它不會二十歲就唱給你聽。多半要過了土星回歸、繞了幾圈冤枉路，你才會突然懂：原來我一直在做的，就是這件事。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合傳給那種一直在問「我活著到底要做什麼」的朋友，提醒他使命不是想出來的，是活出來的。
variations: 你的人生使命不是一句要背起來的座右銘，是你回頭看才發現、一直都在走的那條路。
急著找「我的天命」的人，通常還沒到揭曉的時間——使命是慢慢長出來的，不是查表查出來的。

en.content:
Your Type is about how you do things, your Profile is about how you're seen — and your Incarnation Cross is about why you're here at all.

It's built from just four gates, yet it accounts for roughly 70% of your whole chart. In other words, it's the theme song of your entire life.

The catch: it won't play for you at twenty. Usually it takes a Saturn Return and a few wrong turns before it clicks — oh, the thing I've been doing all along? That was it.
en.editorNote: For the friend endlessly asking 'what am I even here to do.' A reminder that purpose isn't figured out — it's lived into.
variations: Your life purpose isn't a motto to memorize — it's the road you only notice you've been walking once you look back.
If you're rushing to find 'my calling,' it's probably just not reveal time yet. Purpose grows in slowly; you don't look it up in a table.

items.sourceNote: Profile Line 5 — the Heretic; projection field where others project savior expectations onto you; savior-to-scapegoat dynamic; reputation is functional

zh-tw.content:
人生角色裡有「五爻」的人，自帶一種「投射場」：你還沒開口，別人就先幫你腦補了一個角色——救世主、救火隊、什麼問題都能解決的那個人。

爽的是別人天生信任你、把你捧得很高；累的是一旦沒達到那個被幻想出來的期待，同一批人翻臉也翻得最快。

所以五爻的功課是：分清楚別人期待的是「真正的你」，還是他們自己投射出來的影子。答應之前先想清楚，你不需要當每個人的英雄。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那種「總莫名其妙被託付重任、被說很有威嚴」的朋友，幫他理解那份無形的壓力從哪來。
variations: 五爻的人不是愛裝，是別人先把「你一定行」的劇本塞到你手上，你連拒演的機會都沒有。
別人對你的高期待，很多時候不是在看你，是在看他們自己想像出來的你。

en.content:
If you've got a 5th line in your profile, you come with a built-in 'projection field': before you've said a word, people have already cast you in a role — the savior, the fixer, the one who can solve anything.

The upside is instant trust; people put you on a pedestal. The downside is that the moment you don't meet the fantasy they invented, that same crowd turns on you fastest.

So the 5th line's work is telling the difference between what people expect of the real you and the shadow they've projected. Think before you say yes — you don't have to be everyone's hero.
en.editorNote: For the friend who keeps getting handed big responsibilities out of nowhere and told they 'have such authority.' Explain where that invisible pressure comes from.
variations: A 5th line isn't putting on an act — people hand you a 'you've totally got this' script before you ever agreed to play the part.
The high expectations people have of you often aren't about you at all — they're watching the version of you they made up.

items.sourceNote: Profile Line 1 — the Investigator; needs to research to the root and build a solid foundation before feeling secure enough to act; surface answers create anxiety

zh-tw.content:
人生角色有「一爻」的人，是天生的研究控：要先把一件事挖到根、搞懂到底怎麼運作，心裡才踏實。

別人給你一個模稜兩可的答案，你不會安心，只會更焦慮——因為腳下沒有紮實的地基，你根本站不穩。

所以別笑一爻查資料查到半夜、出門前把攻略讀三遍。那不是龜毛，是他打好地基才敢往前走的方式。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那種「做任何決定前一定先做足功課、查到別人受不了」的朋友，幫他正名一下這份謹慎。
variations: 一爻的人不是想太多，是還沒挖到讓自己安心的那塊底才不敢動。
你需要的不是別人叫你「別想那麼多」，是給你時間，把地基打穩再上路。

en.content:
If you've got a 1st line in your profile, you're a born investigator: you have to dig a thing down to its roots and understand exactly how it works before you feel steady inside.

Hand you a vague, hand-wavy answer and it doesn't reassure you — it makes you more anxious, because without solid ground under your feet, you simply can't stand.

So don't laugh at the 1st line researching until 2am or reading the guide three times before leaving the house. That's not being fussy — it's how they lay the foundation before they dare take a step.
en.editorNote: For the friend who does exhaustive homework before any decision, to the point others lose patience. Give that caution its due.
variations: A 1st line isn't overthinking — they just haven't dug down to the bedrock that makes them feel safe enough to move yet.
What you need isn't someone telling you to 'stop thinking so much' — it's time to set the foundation before you hit the road.

items.sourceNote: Design vs Personality — black (conscious mind/who you think you are) vs red (unconscious body/vehicle you didn't design); mind is the passenger, body is the driver

zh-tw.content:
人類圖的圖表有兩種顏色：黑色是「個性」，你以為的自己、你腦袋知道的那個你；紅色是「設計」，你出生前身體就寫好、你自己根本沒發現的那部分。

有個比喻很傳神：紅色是那台車，黑色是坐在後座看風景的乘客。你的腦袋以為自己在開車，其實方向盤一直握在身體手上。

所以人生卡關，常常是因為乘客搶著開車。放手讓身體帶路，你會發現它比你想的還會認路。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合傳給那種「什麼都要用想的、卻越想越亂」的朋友，提醒他腦袋是好副手，但不是好司機。
variations: 你以為是你在做決定，其實很多時候是身體先走了，腦袋才追上來幫它找理由。
腦袋負責欣賞風景，身體負責開車——別讓乘客搶方向盤，車才開得穩。

en.content:
Your Human Design chart comes in two colors: black is your Personality — who you think you are, the you your mind knows. Red is your Design — the part your body was wired with 88 days before birth, the part you've never quite noticed.

There's a great metaphor for it: red is the car, and black is the passenger in the back seat enjoying the view. Your mind assumes it's driving, but the wheel has been in your body's hands the whole time.

So when life gets stuck, it's often the passenger grabbing at the wheel. Let the body lead and you'll find it knows the roads better than you gave it credit for.
en.editorNote: For the friend who tries to think their way through everything and only gets more tangled. A reminder that the mind's a great co-pilot but a lousy driver.
variations: You think you're the one deciding — but a lot of the time the body already moved, and the mind just caught up to invent a reason.
The mind's job is to enjoy the scenery; the body's job is to drive. Don't let the passenger grab the wheel and the ride stays smooth.

items.sourceNote: Signatures (positive counterpart to not-self themes) — Generator satisfaction, Projector success, Manifestor peace, Reflector surprise; the felt sign you're aligned

zh-tw.content:
每種人走錯路會有警報，走對路也有一盞「綠燈」，人類圖叫它 signature：

生產者對了會「滿足」、投射者對了會「成功」、顯示者對了會「平靜」、反映者對了會「驚喜」。

所以與其問「這樣做對不對」，不如回頭感覺一下：我最近是常常滿足、成功、平靜、驚喜，還是常常挫敗、苦澀、憤怒、失望？身體的儀表板不會騙你。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合當作定期自我檢查的提醒，用感受當指南針，比用腦袋硬分析可靠。
variations: 你走對沒走對，不用問別人——問你自己最近是滿足多還是挫敗多就知道了。
順流的訊號很簡單：對的路上你會鬆一口氣，錯的路上你會一直想嘆氣。

en.content:
Every type has an alarm for the wrong path — and a green light for the right one. Human Design calls it your signature.

A Generator on track feels satisfaction, a Projector feels success, a Manifestor feels peace, a Reflector feels surprise.

So instead of asking 'am I doing this right,' check the feeling: lately, have I been mostly satisfied, successful, at peace, pleasantly surprised — or mostly frustrated, bitter, angry, disappointed? The body's dashboard doesn't lie.
en.editorNote: Good as a recurring self-check — using how you feel as the compass, more reliable than grinding it out in your head.
variations: You don't need anyone to tell you if you're on track — just ask yourself whether lately it's been more satisfaction or more frustration.
The sign you're in flow is simple: on the right path you exhale, on the wrong one you keep wanting to sigh.

items.sourceNote: Open/undefined head center (~70% of people) — amplifies mental pressure, takes on others' questions; wisdom lives here once you stop deciding from it

zh-tw.content:
全世界大概七成的人，「腦中心」是空的——這代表你很容易接收別人的疑問，還會自動幫它放大。

所以你半夜睡不著在煩的那些問題，很多根本不是你的：是白天誰隨口一句「你之後打算怎麼辦」，被你收進來養成一隻怪獸。

空的腦中心不是缺陷，是你的智慧來源——前提是你別再拿它做決定，只拿它來欣賞各種問題就好。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那種「腦子停不下來、什麼都要想一輪」的朋友，提醒他先分清楚哪些問題是自己的。
variations: 你不是想太多，是把整個房間的問號都吸進來，一個人扛。
空腦中心的功課：不是每個冒出來的問題，都需要你現在就給答案。

en.content:
Roughly 70% of people have an open Head Center — which means you easily pick up other people's questions and amplify them on autopilot.

So those problems keeping you up at 2am? A lot of them aren't even yours. Someone casually asked 'so what's your plan?' during the day, and you took it home and raised it into a monster.

An open Head isn't a flaw — it's where your wisdom lives, as long as you stop making decisions from it and just enjoy the questions instead.
en.editorNote: For the friend whose brain never shuts off and has to think through everything. A reminder to first sort out which questions are actually theirs.
variations: You're not overthinking — you inhaled every question mark in the room and are carrying them alone.
The open-Head lesson: not every question that pops up needs an answer from you right now.

items.sourceNote: Self-projected authority (Projector, defined G to Throat) — talks it out to hear their own truth; needs a sounding board, not advice

zh-tw.content:
有一種投射者的權威叫「自我投射」，做決定的方式很妙：不是用想的，是用「講出來」的。

他得先把話說出口，聽見自己的聲音，才會知道心裡真正的方向——所以他找你聊，常常不是要你的意見，是要你當那面牆。

如果你身邊有這種人，最好的幫忙就是安靜聽他講完，別急著給建議。他要的答案，在他自己的嘴裡。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那種「一定要講出來才想得清楚」的朋友，也提醒他身邊的人：先聽就好。
variations: 自我投射的人不是話多，是得靠說話幫自己找路。
他找你不是要答案，是要一個安靜的聽眾，讓他聽見自己真正想說的。

en.content:
There's a Projector authority called self-projected, and it makes decisions in a curious way: not by thinking, but by talking it out.

They have to say things out loud and hear their own voice before they know which way they truly lean — so when they come to you, they usually don't want your opinion, they want you to be the wall.

If you've got someone like this in your life, the best help is to shut up and let them finish. Don't rush in with advice. The answer they need is already in their own mouth.
en.editorNote: For the friend who can only think clearly once they've said it out loud — and a reminder to everyone around them: just listen first.
variations: A self-projected type isn't a chatterbox — they talk to find their own way through.
They didn't come for answers. They came for a quiet listener, so they could hear what they actually meant.

items.sourceNote: Ego/Heart authority — the willpower center; decisions run on 'what do I truly want / what's in it for me'; only promise what your heart actually backs

zh-tw.content:
「意志力型」權威的人，做決定的關鍵問句只有一個：我是不是「真心想要」？

聽起來很自私，其實超實際——因為你的心一旦沒真的想要，勉強答應下來的事，最後一定做不完、還會反過來怨自己。

所以別再為了當好人硬撐承諾。你的「不想」不是任性，是身體在提醒你：這件事你撐不到最後。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那種「總是心軟答應、事後又做得很痛苦」的朋友，幫他學會誠實地拒絕。
variations: 意志力型的人只能答應「心裡真的想要」的事，硬撐的承諾遲早會爆掉。
你的「不想做」不是任性，是你的心先幫你踩了煞車。

en.content:
If your authority is ego, or willpower, every decision comes down to one question: do I actually, genuinely want this?

It sounds selfish, but it's brutally practical — because the moment your heart isn't really in it, whatever you forced yourself to agree to won't get finished, and you'll end up resenting yourself for it.

So stop white-knuckling promises just to look like the good guy. Your 'I don't want to' isn't being difficult — it's your body warning you that you can't see this one through.
en.editorNote: For the friend who always caves and says yes, then suffers through it. Help them learn to decline honestly.
variations: A willpower type can only commit to what they truly want — any promise you grind out will eventually blow up.
Your 'I don't want to' isn't stubbornness. It's your heart hitting the brakes for you first.

items.sourceNote: The mind (head + ajna) is the not-self spokesperson — a brilliant outer authority to share wisdom with others, but a terrible inner authority for your own decisions

zh-tw.content:
人類圖有個很反直覺的觀念：你的頭腦不是用來幫「你自己」做決定的。

它是超強的「對外」工具——拿來思考、討論、把想法分享給別人，你的腦袋一級棒；但一輪到你自己的人生選擇，它就開始編一堆「應該、可能、萬一」，把你繞暈。

所以把腦袋放對位置：讓它當你分享給世界的智慧，決定則交給身體。它是很好的顧問，但不是好老闆。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「給別人建議超中肯、輪到自己就當機」的朋友，提醒他腦袋擺對地方就好。
variations: 你的頭腦是拿來照亮別人的燈，不是拿來幫自己選路的方向盤。
難怪你給朋友的建議超準，自己的決定卻老是卡——因為腦袋本來就不是幫你決定的。

en.content:
Human Design has a wonderfully counterintuitive idea: your mind was never meant to make decisions for you.

It's a phenomenal outward tool — for thinking, discussing, sharing ideas with other people, your head is world-class. But the second it's your own life on the line, it starts spinning up a hundred 'shoulds, maybes, and what-ifs' until you're dizzy.

So put the mind where it belongs: let it be the wisdom you offer the world, and hand the deciding to your body. It's a great advisor — just a lousy boss.
en.editorNote: For the friend who gives spot-on advice to everyone but freezes on their own choices. A reminder to just point the mind in the right direction.
variations: Your mind is a lamp meant to light the way for others — not a steering wheel for your own road.
No wonder your advice to friends is dead-on while your own decisions stall — the mind was never built to decide for you.

items.sourceNote: Circuit groups — Individual (mutation/empowerment, focus on self), Tribal (support/resources, focus on close ones), Collective (sharing/understanding, focus on humanity); explains where your attention naturally goes

zh-tw.content:
人類圖裡的「迴路」，講白話就是你的能量天生往哪裡跑。

個人迴路的人，注意力放在「自己」——追求獨特、想帶來改變；部落迴路的人，心繫「身邊的人」——顧家人、顧夥伴、在意資源和支持；集體迴路的人，眼睛看「整個世界」——很想把經驗和知識分享出去。

所以別再嫌那個「只顧自己」的朋友自私、那個「什麼都要管」的朋友愛操心——那不是性格好壞，是三種迴路各有各的任務。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合丟進那種常互相誤會「你怎麼那麼顧自己／你怎麼那麼愛管」的朋友群，幫大家和解一下。
variations: 個人迴路顧自己、部落迴路顧身邊、集體迴路顧全世界——沒有誰比較高尚，是三種人剛好把人類的進化分工做完。
你不是自私，也不是愛管閒事，你只是能量天生流向不同的地方。

en.content:
'Circuitry' in Human Design is basically the direction your energy naturally flows.

Individual-circuit people point their attention at themselves — chasing what's unique, wanting to bring change. Tribal-circuit people focus on their people — family, partners, resources, support. Collective-circuit people look at the whole world — itching to share what they've learned and lived.

So quit calling the 'only-thinks-about-themselves' friend selfish, or the 'has-to-manage-everything' friend a control freak. It's not good character versus bad — each circuit just has a different job.
en.editorNote: Drop it in the group chat that keeps misreading each other as 'so self-absorbed' or 'such a control freak.' A little peace treaty.
variations: Individual circuit tends to itself, tribal to its people, collective to all of humanity — nobody's nobler; the three just split up the work of evolving our species.
You're not selfish and you're not a busybody — your energy is just wired to flow somewhere different.

items.sourceNote: Environmental / Sounding-Board authority (Projector with no defined center below the throat) — clarity comes not from a body signal but from the right physical setting plus talking it out with trusted people

zh-tw.content:
有一種投射者沒有「內在權威」，做決定不是靠身體給訊號，是靠「環境」和「把話講給對的人聽」。

同一個決定，你在吵雜的咖啡廳想破頭都沒答案，換到安靜的散步路上、講給信任的人聽，突然就清楚了。

所以別逼自己坐在原地硬想，也別急著當場給答覆——換個地方、找個對的人聊一聊，你要的清晰，是「聊出來、走出來」的。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「換個環境就想通、悶在房間反而卡死」的投射者朋友，提醒他決定要靠對的場合。
variations: 你不是優柔寡斷，你只是還沒到對的地方、還沒跟對的人聊過。
環境型權威的人：答案不在腦袋裡，在那條散步的路上、那個懂你的人面前。

en.content:
Some Projectors have no inner authority at all — they don't decide from a body signal, they decide through their environment and by talking it out with the right people.

The same decision you can't crack in a loud café suddenly clears up on a quiet walk, said out loud to someone you trust.

So stop forcing it while sitting still, and don't hand over an answer on the spot. Change your setting, talk to the right person — the clarity you want gets talked out and walked out.
en.editorNote: For the Projector friend who figures everything out the moment they change scenery but stalls stewing in one room. The decision needs the right setting.
variations: You're not indecisive — you just haven't gotten to the right place or talked to the right person yet.
Environmental authority: the answer isn't in your head. It's on that walk, in front of the person who gets you.

items.sourceNote: Lunar authority (Reflector) — the only type with no inner authority; decisions ripen over a full 28-day lunar cycle while talking with trusted people and sampling environments

zh-tw.content:
反映者是唯一「沒有內在權威」的人，他們的權威在天上——月亮。

重大決定要等一整輪月亮週期（大約 28 天），中間不斷跟信任的人聊、去不同的地方感受，讓答案慢慢浮現，急不得。

所以如果你身邊有反映者，別逼他當天給你答覆。他不是在拖，是他的設計就是要用一個月，把這件事從頭到尾照過一遍。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合傳給正在等反映者朋友回覆的人，也提醒反映者自己：重大決定，值得等滿一輪月亮。
variations: 反映者做決定的節奏，是跟著月亮走的：月圓月缺跑完一輪，答案才算數。
別催反映者。他要的不是更快，是把每個角度、每種心情都走過一遍。

en.content:
Reflectors are the only type with no inner authority — theirs hangs in the sky. It's the moon.

Big decisions take a full lunar cycle (about 28 days), and all the way through it they keep talking things over with people they trust and sampling different places, letting the answer surface slowly. No rushing it.

So if there's a Reflector in your life, don't push them to answer same-day. They're not stalling — they're literally built to take a month to view the whole thing from start to finish.
en.editorNote: For anyone waiting on a Reflector's reply — and a reminder to Reflectors: a big decision deserves a full turn of the moon.
variations: A Reflector decides on the moon's schedule: only once it's gone full to new and back does the answer count.
Don't rush a Reflector. They don't want it faster — they want to have walked through every angle and every mood.

items.sourceNote: Individual circuit — theme of mutation and empowerment; being different is the design, carries a built-in melancholy that precedes creativity; not fitting in is the function, not a flaw

zh-tw.content:
個人迴路重的人，天生就是「不合群」的那一個：想法怪、時機怪、連走的路都跟大家不一樣。

這不是叛逆，是設計——個人迴路的任務就是「突變」，帶來別人想不到的新東西。代價是它自帶一股憂鬱，像創作前的那種低氣壓，你得學會跟它共處，而不是急著把它趕走。

所以別再逼自己「正常一點」。你的與眾不同，正是這世界需要你帶來的那一味。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「從小就覺得自己格格不入」的朋友，告訴他那不是毛病，是天賦。
variations: 你不是怪，你是負責幫人類「突變」的那個人——怪，剛好是你的功能。
個人迴路的憂鬱不是壞掉，是好東西來之前的那陣低氣壓，等它過，靈感就到了。

en.content:
If your chart is heavy on individual circuitry, you're the one who's wired to not fit in: odd ideas, odd timing, a path that just doesn't match everyone else's.

That's not rebellion — it's design. The individual circuit's job is mutation, bringing the new thing nobody else saw coming. The price tag is a built-in melancholy, that low-pressure hum before creation. You learn to sit with it instead of chasing it off.

So stop trying to be 'more normal.' Your not-fitting-in is exactly the flavor this world needs you to bring.
en.editorNote: For the friend who's felt like an outsider since childhood. Tell them it's not a defect — it's the gift.
variations: You're not weird — you're the one assigned to mutate the species. Weird is just the job description.
Individual-circuit melancholy isn't a malfunction — it's the low pressure before creativity rolls in. Wait it out and the good stuff arrives.

items.sourceNote: Open/undefined Sacral center — no fixed life-force energy, doesn't know when enough is enough, overworks and pushes past its limit trying to keep up with defined-Sacral people

zh-tw.content:
薦骨空白的人，身體沒有內建「夠了」這個訊號。

所以你會一直做、一直撐，直到累到癱了才發現早就過頭——因為你根本感覺不到那條界線在哪。

你不是懶，剛好相反，你是那種「不知道什麼時候該停」的人。學會提早收手，不是偷懶，是保命。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個總是把自己操到爆、還覺得「別人都可以我怎麼不行」的朋友，他缺的不是意志力，是一個煞車。
variations: 薦骨空白的座右銘不該是「再撐一下」，而是「我今天到這裡就好」。
你不是不夠努力，你是沒有那個「該休息了」的體內鬧鐘，得自己手動設一個。

en.content:
If your Sacral center is open, your body has no built-in 'that's enough' signal.

So you keep going, keep pushing, until you collapse and realize you blew past your limit ages ago — because you literally can't feel where the line is.

You're not lazy. If anything it's the opposite: you're the type who doesn't know when to stop. Learning to quit early isn't slacking — it's survival.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who grinds themselves into the ground and still thinks 'everyone else can handle it, why can't I?' They don't need more willpower — they need a brake pedal.
variations: An open-Sacral motto shouldn't be 'just a little more.' It should be 'I'm done for today.'
You're not under-trying — you just don't have the internal 'time to rest' alarm. You have to set one manually.

items.sourceNote: Open/undefined Root center — amplifies adrenal pressure from the environment; other people's deadlines feel like personal emergencies; rushes to get free of pressure

zh-tw.content:
根部空白的人，會把周圍所有的「趕」都吸進來還放大。

別人的死線變成你的心悸，尖峰時刻的人潮讓你莫名焦躁，群組跳出一句「在嗎急」你整個人瞬間繃緊。

你不是抗壓性差，你是把全世界的壓力都免費接收了。學會問一句「這個急，是我的，還是我接來的？」，你會鬆一大半。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「一被催就慌、事後才發現根本沒那麼急」的朋友，提醒他先分清楚壓力的產權。
variations: 你以為的「我壓力好大」，有一半是旁邊那個人的壓力，只是剛好被你接住了。
根部空白的人最需要的一句話：不是每一個「急」，都值得你跟著急。

en.content:
If your Root center is open, you soak up every bit of 'hurry' around you — and amplify it.

Someone else's deadline becomes your heart palpitations. Rush-hour crowds leave you inexplicably wired. One 'you there? it's urgent' in the group chat and your whole body snaps tight.

You're not bad at handling pressure — you're just receiving the entire world's pressure for free. Learn to ask 'is this urgency mine, or did I just pick it up?' and half of it lets go.
en.editorNote: For the friend who panics the second someone rushes them, then realizes later it was never that urgent. A reminder to check the ownership of the pressure first.
variations: Half of what feels like 'I'm so stressed' is the stress of the person next to you — you just happened to catch it.
The line an open-Root person needs most: not every 'urgent' deserves you getting urgent too.

items.sourceNote: Open/undefined Heart/Ego/Will center — no consistent willpower or self-worth; makes promises to prove worth, over-commits to prove it's enough; lesson is worth isn't something to prove

zh-tw.content:
意志力中心空白的人，最容易掉進一個坑：拚命證明「我夠好、我有價值」。

所以你會亂許承諾、硬扛不屬於你的責任、答應了做不到的事——只為了讓別人覺得你可靠。

但你的價值從來不需要用「做到什麼」去換。你光是存在，就已經夠了，不用靠一張又一張的承諾去買。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「總是逞強答應、事後累死自己」的朋友，提醒他不用一直證明什麼。
variations: 你不需要靠「我一定做到」來換別人的肯定，你本來就值得。
意志力空白的人要練的不是更強的意志力，是「不用證明也沒關係」的安心。

en.content:
If your Heart center is open, there's one trap you fall into again and again: desperately proving 'I'm good enough, I have worth.'

So you make promises you shouldn't, shoulder responsibilities that aren't yours, say yes to things you can't deliver — all so people will see you as dependable.

But your worth was never something to trade for 'what you got done.' Just by existing, you're already enough. You don't have to buy it back one promise at a time.
en.editorNote: For the friend who over-commits to look strong and then wrecks themselves following through. A reminder they have nothing to prove.
variations: You don't have to earn approval with 'I'll definitely pull it off.' You were already worth it.
An open-Heart person doesn't need stronger willpower — they need the calm of knowing they don't have to prove anything.

items.sourceNote: Open/undefined Solar Plexus center — absorbs and amplifies everyone's emotions, avoids confrontation to keep the peace; once decoupled, becomes deeply wise about human feeling

zh-tw.content:
情緒中心空白的人，一走進房間就知道現場氣氛對不對——因為你把每個人的情緒都收進來還放大了。

所以你超怕衝突、超會看臉色、寧可自己吞下去也不想讓場面尷尬。別人一句話語氣重一點，你能難過一整晚。

但這不是玻璃心，是天線太靈。等你學會「這情緒不是我的」，你會變成那個最懂別人、最會安慰人的存在。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「聚會上最會察言觀色、回家卻累到不行」的高敏感朋友，告訴他那是天賦不是弱點。
variations: 你不是想太多，你是把整個房間的情緒都接收進來了。
情緒空白的人最強的超能力：先分清楚「這份難過，到底是我的，還是我接來的」。

en.content:
If your Solar Plexus is open, you walk into a room and instantly know if the vibe's off — because you've absorbed everyone's emotions and amplified them.

So you dread conflict, read every face, and would rather swallow it than let things get awkward. One slightly sharp tone from someone and you're sad the whole night.

But that's not a fragile ego — your antenna is just extremely sensitive. Once you learn 'this feeling isn't mine,' you become the person who understands others best and comforts them like no one else.
en.editorNote: For the highly-sensitive friend who reads the whole room at a party then comes home completely drained. Tell them it's a gift, not a weakness.
variations: You're not overthinking — you just received the emotions of the entire room.
An open-Solar-Plexus superpower: sorting out 'is this sadness actually mine, or did I just pick it up?'

items.sourceNote: Open/undefined Head center — mental pressure center; feels pressure to answer questions and solve inspirations that aren't even relevant to them; not-self is being occupied with questions that don't matter

zh-tw.content:
頭腦中心空白的人，腦袋常常被一堆「不是自己的問題」塞爆。

看到一則新聞就想「這該怎麼辦」、聽到別人的煩惱就自動幫他想解方、半夜還在糾結一個根本跟你無關的疑問。

那些壓力其實不是你的功課。學會問一句「這個問題，值得我花腦力嗎？」，你會發現大部分都可以直接放生。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合那種「腦袋停不下來、什麼都想插手想通」的朋友，提醒他不是每個問題都需要他來解。
variations: 你不是愛想，是接收了一堆別人的問題還當成自己的作業在寫。
頭腦空白的人要練的是「篩選」：不是每個冒出來的問題，都值得你熬夜想。

en.content:
If your Head center is open, your mind gets crammed with questions that were never yours to answer.

You read one headline and think 'what should be done about this,' hear someone else's problem and auto-generate solutions, lie awake at 2 a.m. chewing on a question that has nothing to do with you.

That pressure isn't your homework. Learn to ask 'is this question even worth my brainpower?' and you'll find most of them can just be released into the wild.
en.editorNote: For the friend whose mind never stops, who tries to figure out and fix everything. A reminder that not every question is theirs to solve.
variations: You don't love overthinking — you just took in a pile of other people's questions and started doing them like assigned homework.
The open-Head skill to practice is filtering: not every question that pops up is worth losing sleep over.

items.sourceNote: Sleep by type — open/undefined sacral (Projector, Manifestor, Reflector) should lie down before feeling tired and sleep out of others' auras; defined sacral should burn energy off before bed

zh-tw.content:
人類圖給非能量型的睡眠建議，聽起來很叛逆：在你「還沒累」之前就去躺著。

因為薦骨空白的人是靠躺平放電，不是靠累到爆才睡。躺著滑個手機、看兩頁書，把白天吸進來的雜訊排掉，才睡得沉。

反過來，薦骨有定義的人如果沒把當天的能量用完就上床，只會躺著翻來覆去到半夜。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合傳給那個「明明很累卻睡不著」的朋友，也適合替自己早早上床找一個很有說服力的理由。
variations: 空白薦骨的睡前守則：先躺，不用等累。累了才躺，就太晚了。
睡不著有兩種：一種是能量沒放完，一種是別人的能量還在你身上沒退。

en.content:
Human Design's sleep advice for non-energy types sounds like heresy: lie down before you feel tired.

An undefined Sacral discharges by lying down, not by running itself into the ground first. Get horizontal, scroll a bit, read two pages, let the day's static drain off — then you actually sleep.

Flip side: if your Sacral is defined and you get into bed without burning off the day's energy, you'll just be staring at the ceiling until 2 a.m.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who's exhausted but can't fall asleep — and keep it handy as a very convincing excuse to go to bed early yourself.
variations: Undefined Sacral bedtime rule: lie down first, don't wait for tired. If you're already tired, you're late.
There are two kinds of insomnia: energy you didn't burn off, and energy that wasn't yours to begin with.

items.sourceNote: Profile Line 2 — the Hermit; natural talent developed in private, doesn't see it as special, needs to be called out rather than self-promote

zh-tw.content:
二爻「隱士」的天賦是在家裡默默長出來的，而且他自己完全不覺得那有什麼了不起。

「這誰不會啊？」——不，只有你會，而且你是隨手就會。

二爻不是來自我推銷的，是來被叫出門的。你只要專心把自己養好，那個看見你的人自然會來敲門。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合傳給那種「明明很強卻超級低調、還一直說自己沒什麼」的朋友，順便逼他接受稱讚。
variations: 二爻的日常：關起門來練成絕活，開門被問「你怎麼會這個」，然後一臉困惑。
你不是不夠好，你只是還沒被叫出來。躲著，也是一種準備。

en.content:
A Line 2 Hermit grows their talent quietly at home — and genuinely doesn't think it's a big deal.

'Doesn't everyone know how to do this?' No. Only you. And you do it without even trying.

A Hermit isn't built to self-promote. They're built to be called out. Just keep tending your own thing; the person who sees it will come knocking.
en.editorNote: For the friend who's absurdly good at something and insists it's nothing. Bonus points if it forces them to accept a compliment.
variations: A Hermit's whole life: perfect a skill behind a closed door, open it, get asked 'how do you even know how to do that,' look genuinely confused.
You're not underqualified. You just haven't been called out yet. Hiding is part of the prep.

items.sourceNote: Profile Line 4 — the Opportunist; opportunities arrive through the existing network, and the 4th line needs the next thing lined up before letting go of the current one

zh-tw.content:
四爻的機會從來不是投履歷投來的，是「認識的人」帶來的。

而且四爻有個很誠實的習慣：一定要先接好下一根繩子，才肯放開手上這一根。新工作沒談定不會離職，下一段沒著落不會先走。

這不是騎驢找馬的心虛，是四爻的原廠設計——他天生要有橋才過河。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合傳給那個「工作機會都是朋友介紹」的人，也可以拿來安慰不敢裸辭的自己。
variations: 四爻的求職法：不是打開人力銀行，是打開通訊錄。
四爻不是優柔寡斷，是不做沒有落點的跳躍。

en.content:
A Line 4's opportunities never come from cold applications. They come from people who already know them.

And a 4th line has one very honest habit: they won't let go of the rope in hand until the next one's secured. No resignation without the offer signed. No exit without the next thing lined up.

That's not cowardice. It's the factory setting — a 4th line needs a bridge before they'll cross the river.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend whose every job came through a referral, or keep it for the next time you feel guilty about not quitting on a whim.
variations: A Line 4 doesn't job-hunt on job boards. They job-hunt in their contacts list.
A 4th line isn't indecisive — they just refuse to jump without knowing where they'll land.

items.sourceNote: Quadruple split definition — the rarest definition type (~1%); four separate circuits of defined centers; needs the most time and the right people/environment to feel integrated

zh-tw.content:
「四分人」是定義類型裡最稀有的，大概只有 1%：身上有定義的能量中心，被切成四塊各自運作。

所以他常常覺得自己好像四個人在同一個身體裡開會，而且開很久都沒結論。

四分人不是難搞，是需要更多人、更多場合、更多時間，那四塊才會慢慢接起來。給他時間，他會整合得比誰都完整。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合傳給那種「跟不同人相處像不同的人、決定又特別慢」的朋友，讓他知道那是設計，不是缺陷。
variations: 四分人做決定的速度慢，是因為要等四個內部部門都表決完。
你不是人格分裂，你只是內建了四條沒接起來的線路。

en.content:
Quadruple split is the rarest definition type — around 1% of people. Your defined centers come in four separate circuits, each running on its own.

Which is why it can feel like four people holding a meeting inside one body, and the meeting never reaches a decision.

A quadruple split isn't difficult. They just need more people, more settings, and more time before those four pieces link up. Give them that, and they integrate more completely than anyone.
en.editorNote: For the friend who feels like a different person with every group and takes forever to decide. It's the design, not a defect.
variations: A quadruple split decides slowly because four internal departments all have to finish voting first.
You're not scattered. You're just wired with four circuits that haven't been connected yet.

items.sourceNote: Transits — the daily planetary movement temporarily colors in open centers, bridges splits, and pulses defined channels; explains sudden mood/energy shifts that aren't really 'you'

zh-tw.content:
有些日子你莫名其妙特別焦躁、特別多話、或特別想衝——先別急著檢討自己。

人類圖裡，天上的行星每天都在移動，會臨時把你「空白的中心」暫時塗滿。那個突如其來的情緒，很可能不是你的，是今天的天空借你穿一下。

所以與其問「我今天怎麼了」，不如問「這是我的，還是路過的」。是路過的，過幾天就還回去了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個「今天心情很差但講不出原因」的朋友，讓他知道有時候真的不是他的問題，是流日。
variations: 你不是情緒不穩，你只是空白中心今天被天空租走了。
有些壞心情不用解決，等它退潮就好——那本來就不是你的。

en.content:
Some days you're weirdly restless, extra chatty, or itching to charge at everything — before you put yourself on trial, wait.

In Human Design, the planets overhead move every single day, and they temporarily color in your open centers. That mood that came out of nowhere? Often it isn't yours — it's just today's sky borrowing your body for a bit.

So instead of 'what's wrong with me today,' ask 'is this mine, or is it just passing through?' If it's passing through, it'll hand itself back in a few days.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who's 'in a terrible mood for no reason.' Sometimes it genuinely isn't them — it's the transit.
variations: You're not unstable. Your open centers just got rented out by the sky today.
Some bad moods don't need fixing — they need low tide. They were never yours to begin with.

items.sourceNote: Parenting by type — a Projector child has no defined Sacral and can't keep the pace of a Generator child; forcing them to 'keep up' breeds bitterness; recognize and invite instead of push

zh-tw.content:
如果你家小孩是投射者，別再叫他「跟哥哥一樣去跑一跑放電」——他根本沒有那顆會源源不絕的薦骨電池，硬撐只會累到崩潰。

投射者小孩要的是「被看見、被邀請」：你多稱讚他觀察到的細節，他會亮起來；你老是催他快一點，他會慢慢變得苦澀。

生產者小孩才是拿去放電的，投射者小孩是拿來好好對話的。用錯方式帶，不是孩子有問題，是說明書拿反了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那種「兩個小孩差超多、老是拿來比」的爸媽，提醒他們不同款要用不同的帶法。
variations: 投射者小孩不是懶，是沒有無限電力；催他快，只會養出一個苦澀的大人。
帶生產者小孩用『放電』，帶投射者小孩用『看見』——用錯就兩敗俱傷。

en.content:
If your kid's a Projector, stop telling them to 'go run it off like your brother does' — they simply don't have the endless Sacral battery, and forcing it just burns them out.

A Projector child runs on being seen and invited. Praise the detail they noticed and they light up. Keep rushing them to hurry, and they slowly turn bitter.

Generator kids are the ones you send outside to burn energy. Projector kids are the ones you sit down and really talk with. Parent them wrong and it's not the kid who's broken — the manual's just upside down.
en.editorNote: For the parents with two very different kids who keep comparing them. Different design, different manual.
variations: A Projector kid isn't lazy — they just don't have infinite power. Rush them and you raise a bitter adult.
Generator kids need to burn energy; Projector kids need to be seen. Swap the two and everyone loses.

items.sourceNote: Channel 59-6 (Mating/Intimacy) — Gate 59 uniquely penetrates and dissolves auric barriers; natural ice-breakers who create instant closeness, but the lesson is to let others invite it, not intrude

zh-tw.content:
如果你身上有「59-6 親密通道」，你大概就是那種——認識三分鐘，別人就把心事全掏給你的人。

人類圖裡，只有 59 號閘門有一個特殊能力：它會直接穿透別人的氣場防線。所以你不用刻意，天生就會拆牆、破冰、讓人卸下防備。

但天賦的另一面是：你也很容易「太快靠近」。功課不是關掉這份親近力，是學會等對方先邀請你進來，而不是自己直接推門。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個「跟誰都能瞬間熟起來、但也常越界」的朋友，讓他知道那是天賦也是要拿捏的分寸。
variations: 59-6 的人不是自來熟，是天生會拆牆——但牆是別人的，最好等他自己開門。
你的親和力是穿透氣場的等級，威力太強，記得先等邀請再靠近。

en.content:
If you've got the 59-6 (the Mating/Intimacy channel), you're probably the person strangers spill their whole life story to after three minutes.

In Human Design, Gate 59 has a power no other gate has: it penetrates straight through other people's auric barriers. You don't even try — you're wired to dissolve walls, break ice, and get people to drop their guard.

The flip side: you also close distance really fast. The lesson isn't to switch off the closeness — it's to let the other person invite you in, instead of pushing the door open yourself.
en.editorNote: For the friend who bonds with anyone instantly but sometimes oversteps. It's a gift and a boundary lesson at once.
variations: A 59-6 isn't nosy — they're built to dissolve walls. But the wall is someone else's; wait for them to open the door.
Your warmth operates at aura-penetrating strength. Powerful stuff — wait for the invite before you close in.

items.sourceNote: Beginner entry point — don't drown in 64 gates and 36 channels; the whole practical experiment starts with just Type + Strategy + Authority; everything else is layered on later

zh-tw.content:
剛入坑人類圖，看到 64 個閘門、36 條通道、九大中心，直接被勸退——其實你不用一次吃完整本說明書。

真正要先搞懂的只有三件事：你的「類型」（決定你的能量怎麼運作）、「策略」（決定你怎麼開始一件事）、「內在權威」（決定你怎麼做決定）。

光是照這三樣過生活，你就已經在做人類圖的實驗了。其他那些花俏的名詞，等你有感覺了再慢慢加上去也不遲。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個「很想研究人類圖但一看到圖就頭痛」的朋友，給他一個不會被勸退的起點。
variations: 人類圖新手三件事：類型、策略、內在權威。剩下的先別急，會嚇跑你。
你不用背完 64 個閘門才開始——先照著類型、策略、權威過一週再說。

en.content:
New to Human Design and instantly scared off by 64 gates, 36 channels, and nine centers? Good news: you don't have to swallow the whole manual at once.

There are really only three things to get first: your Type (how your energy is meant to run), your Strategy (how you're meant to start things), and your Authority (how you're meant to make decisions).
