Live by just those three and you're already running the Human Design experiment. All the fancier terminology can get layered on later, once you actually feel something.
en.editorNote: For the friend who wants to get into Human Design but gets a headache the second they see the chart. Here's a starting point that won't scare them off.
variations: Human Design for beginners, three things only: Type, Strategy, Authority. Leave the rest — it'll spook you.
You don't need all 64 gates memorized to start. Live by Type, Strategy, and Authority for a week first.

items.sourceNote: The Six Motivations (Fear, Hope, Desire, Need, Guilt, Innocence) — the deep 'why' behind how your mind reaches out to others; each has a not-self transference that quietly flips it into its opposite

zh-tw.content:
人類圖裡有個很少人講、但超好用的東西叫「動機」——就是你內心深處，做每件事背後那個真正的引擎。

總共六種：恐懼、希望、慾望、需求、罪惡感、天真。你不是「有」其中一種，是你整張圖都被那個顏色染過一遍。

最狠的是：壓力一大，動機會自動翻面。天真的人變得算計、希望的人變得絕望、慾望的人變得放棄。所以與其問「我在做什麼」，不如問「我到底是為了什麼在做」——答對了，人就鬆了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那種「什麼都做了卻還是覺得不對勁」的朋友，問題常常不在做的事，而在背後那個動機翻面了。
variations: 恐懼、希望、慾望、需求、罪惡感、天真——這六個動機不是你選的，是你出廠自帶的底色。
壓力一來，你的動機會偷偷翻成反面。累的不是事情本身，是你為了錯的理由在做對的事。

en.content:
There's a rarely-talked-about but wildly useful layer in Human Design called your Motivation — the real engine underneath everything you do.

There are six: Fear, Hope, Desire, Need, Guilt, and Innocence. You don't just 'have' one — your whole chart gets tinted by it.

The brutal part: under pressure, your motivation flips to its opposite. Innocence turns scheming, Hope turns hopeless, Desire turns to giving up. So instead of asking 'what am I doing,' ask 'what am I actually doing it for.' Get that right and your whole body relaxes.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who's done all the right things and still feels off. Usually the problem isn't the thing they're doing — it's that the motivation underneath flipped to its shadow.
variations: Fear, Hope, Desire, Need, Guilt, Innocence — you didn't pick your motivation, it shipped with you as the base coat.
Under pressure your motivation quietly flips to its opposite. What's draining you isn't the task — it's doing the right thing for the wrong reason.

items.sourceNote: Aura mechanics scene — all five aura types thrown into one party; open/enveloping (Generator), busy/tasting (MG), focused/absorbing (Projector), closed/repelling (Manifestor), sampling/resistant (Reflector); comedic but mechanically accurate

zh-tw.content:
把五種人類圖類型丟進同一場聚會，氣場現場開打：

生產者站在食物旁邊，開放氣場把整桌人都吸過去，不知不覺變成中心。顯示生產者一晚換三個圈子，每桌都待五分鐘就走。投射者角落一杯酒，把每個人都看穿，聊三小時後隔天要躺平一整天。

顯示者一進門氣場往外推，大家自動讓路，還以為他很兇（他只是想安靜喝完這杯）。反映者站在門口，整場的氣氛好不好，看他臉就知道——他是這場派對的體溫計。
zh-tw.editorNote: 適合配上一張聚會照，tag 那群你已經看出誰是哪一型的朋友，準到會被打。
variations: 同一場派對：生產者被吸成中心、MG 到處亂竄、投射者看穿全場、顯示者自帶結界、反映者當溫度計。
怎麼在聚會認出人類圖類型？看氣場就好——被圍住的、待不住的、在角落看穿你的、大家自動讓路的、還有那個站門口的溫度計。

en.content:
Throw all five Human Design types into one party and watch the auras go to war:

The Generator posts up by the snacks, their open aura pulling the whole room in until they're accidentally the center of it. The Manifesting Generator cycles through three friend groups a night, five minutes per table, gone. The Projector nurses one drink in the corner, reads everyone to the bone, talks for three hours, then needs the entire next day horizontal.

The Manifestor walks in and their aura pushes outward — people instinctively part like they're intimidating (they just want to finish this drink in peace). The Reflector stands by the door, and whether the party's actually good? Just check their face. They're the room's thermometer.
en.editorNote: Pair with a party photo and tag the friends whose types you've already clocked. Accurate enough to get you smacked.
variations: One party: the Generator gets pulled into being the center, the MG can't sit still, the Projector reads the whole room, the Manifestor has a forcefield, and the Reflector is the thermometer.

How to spot Human Design types at a party? Watch the auras — the one everyone circles, the one who can't stay put, the one reading you from the corner, the one people part for, and the thermometer by the door.

items.sourceNote: Manifestor informing in relationships — informing is NOT asking permission; it removes the resistance/anger loop; the couple reframe of 'I'm telling you so you're not blindsided, not asking if I may'

zh-tw.content:
顯示者在感情裡最容易被誤會的一句話是：「我等等要出門一下。」

伴侶常聽成「他在報備」或「他在問我可不可以」，其實兩個都不是。顯示者的策略是「告知」——不是請求許可，是先講一聲，讓你不會被突襲。

差別很大：被要求許可的顯示者會生悶氣（那是他的非自己主題「憤怒」），但只是先講一聲的顯示者，反而願意主動說。你不用批准他，你只需要知道。搞懂這一點，這段關係會少掉一半的火。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給正在跟顯示者交往、常覺得「他怎麼都不商量」的人——他不是不在乎你，他只是天生要用告知的方式，不是討論的方式。
variations: 顯示者說「我要出門」不是在問你，是在告知你。你不用批准，你只需要知道——這樣他就不會有阻力。
跟顯示者相處的關鍵：他先講一聲不是報備，是策略。你要他報備討論，他反而會關機。

en.content:
The most misread sentence a Manifestor says in a relationship: 'Hey, I'm heading out for a bit.'

Partners tend to hear either 'he's reporting to me' or 'he's asking if he can.' It's neither. A Manifestor's strategy is to inform — not ask permission, just give a heads-up so you're not blindsided.

The difference is huge: a Manifestor made to ask permission goes quietly furious (anger is literally their not-self theme). But a Manifestor who just gets to inform will actually volunteer it freely. You don't need to approve them — you just need to be in the loop. Get this and you cut half the friction out of the relationship.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone dating a Manifestor who keeps thinking 'why won't he ever run things by me' — he's not shutting you out, he's just wired to inform, not to negotiate.
variations: When a Manifestor says 'I'm heading out,' he's not asking — he's informing. You don't approve it, you're just in the loop. That's what keeps his resistance down.
The key to loving a Manifestor: the heads-up isn't him reporting in, it's his strategy. Demand a discussion instead and he'll just power down.

items.sourceNote: PHS Environment variable — the six environments (Cave, Market, Kitchen, Mountain, Valley, Shore); where the body focuses and functions best, not a rigid rule about where to live

zh-tw.content:
人類圖裡最容易被忽略、但一用就有感的設定，是你的「環境」——你在哪裡最能靜下心、最有效率。

分成六種：洞穴（要有安全感的封閉小空間）、市場（人來人往才有靈感）、廚房（有製造、有火、有動靜的地方）、山（居高臨下、看得遠）、谷（走廊、通道、連接彼此的地方）、岸（在兩種狀態的交界，像海跟陸之間）。

所以有人在咖啡廳吵成一團反而效率爆表，有人非得躲進沒人的房間才寫得出東西——不是誰比較嬌，是你們天生要待的地方就不一樣。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個一直逼自己在開放辦公室工作、卻越坐越煩的朋友——他可能是「洞穴」型，換個角落人生就順了。
variations: 別再羨慕別人在星巴克工作很帥。你是洞穴型的話，硬待在市場只會漏電。
人類圖的六種環境：洞穴、市場、廚房、山、谷、岸。找到你的，效率直接翻倍。

en.content:
The most overlooked-but-instantly-useful setting in Human Design is your Environment — the kind of space where you actually focus and feel right.

There are six: Cave (a safe, enclosed, controlled space), Market (needs the buzz of people passing through), Kitchen (somewhere with activity, heat, things being made), Mountain (up high, with the long view), Valley (hallways, corridors, places that connect people), and Shore (the edge where two conditions meet, like where sea meets land).

So some people do their best work in a loud café while others can't write a word unless they're hidden in an empty room. Nobody's being precious — you're just wired for different places.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who keeps forcing themselves to work in an open office and getting crankier by the hour — they might be a Cave, and one corner change fixes everything.
variations: Stop envying people who look cool working at Starbucks. If you're a Cave, sitting in a Market just drains you.
The six Human Design Environments: Cave, Market, Kitchen, Mountain, Valley, Shore. Find yours and your focus doubles.

items.sourceNote: PHS Cognition / Sense variable — the six cognitions (Smell, Taste, Outer Vision, Inner Vision, Feeling, Touch); your 'super sense', the way you most reliably take in and vet information

zh-tw.content:
人類圖還有一個叫「認知」的設定，就是你天生最靈的那個感官——有人叫它「超級感官」。

六種：嗅覺（靠聞味道判斷對不對，第一印象特別準）、味覺（要慢慢品、細細分辨才有答案）、外視覺（看外在畫面、環境、氛圍才懂）、內視覺（閉上眼在腦內想像、預演才清楚）、感覺（靠情緒跟氛圍的溫度來讀世界）、觸覺（一定要親身碰到、實際體驗過才算數）。

所以有人買東西一定要摸過、有人光聞就知道這人不能深交、有人得回家閉眼想一晚才敢下決定——都不是龜毛，是各自的感官在替他們把關。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個逛街一定要摸過每件衣服的朋友——他不是奧客，他是「觸覺」型認知，摸到才有資料可以判斷。
variations: 有人一聞就知道不對勁，有人要摸過才安心，有人得閉眼想一晚。六種認知，各有各的雷達。
你天生最準的那個感官，人類圖叫它「超級感官」：嗅、味、外視、內視、感覺、觸覺，總有一個是你的主力。

en.content:
Human Design also has a setting called Cognition — your sharpest, most trustworthy sense. Some people call it your 'super sense.'

Six flavors: Smell (you literally 'sniff out' whether something's right; killer first impressions), Taste (you need to savor and discern slowly before you know), Outer Vision (you read the world through what you see out there — the scene, the vibe), Inner Vision (you need to close your eyes and picture it, run the movie in your head), Feeling (you read the emotional temperature of a room), and Touch (it doesn't count until you've physically touched it or lived it).

So one person has to handle every item before buying, another can smell that someone's shady, another needs a night with their eyes closed before deciding. Nobody's being difficult — their sense is just doing the vetting.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who has to touch every item on the rack — they're not being annoying, they're Touch cognition, and they literally can't gather data any other way.
variations: One friend smells trouble instantly, one has to touch it first, one needs to sleep on it with eyes closed. Six cognitions, six radars.
Your sharpest sense — Human Design calls it your 'super sense': Smell, Taste, Outer Vision, Inner Vision, Feeling, or Touch. One of them is your main antenna.

items.sourceNote: Perspective / View variable (bottom-right arrow, Personality side) — Focused/left sees the details (one tree), Peripheral/right sees the whole forest; explains detail-people vs big-picture-people

zh-tw.content:
為什麼有人天生只看細節，有人天生只看大局？人類圖有個叫「視角」的設定在管這件事。

分兩種：聚焦型（左）——眼睛自動停在細節上，看到的是那棵樹、那根樹枝、那片葉子的顏色，重點、瑕疵、小地方都逃不過他；周邊型（右）——看的是整片森林、是氛圍、是邊邊角角在發生什麼，全局感很強，但你叫他找出那顆打錯的字他會崩潰。

所以校稿要交給聚焦型，規劃大方向要交給周邊型。別再嫌另一半「怎麼都不看細節」或「怎麼都不看大局」——你們的雷達本來就對焦在不同地方。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那對一個嫌對方龜毛、一個嫌對方粗線條的情侶——不是誰有問題，是視角天生一個看樹、一個看林。
variations: 校稿找聚焦型，規劃找周邊型。硬要對調，兩個都會抓狂。
一個看樹、一個看林。人類圖的「視角」設定，解釋了你跟另一半為什麼永遠在意不一樣的東西。

en.content:
Why is one person wired to see only the details and another only the big picture? Human Design has a setting for that, called Perspective (or View).

Two modes: Focused (left) — the eye lands on details, sees that one tree, that branch, the exact color of that leaf; nothing small or flawed gets past them. Peripheral (right) — sees the whole forest, the flow, what's happening at the edges; huge on context, but ask them to spot the one typo and they'll melt down.

So hand the proofreading to a Focused person and the big-picture planning to a Peripheral one. Stop grumbling that your partner 'never notices details' or 'never sees the big picture' — your radars are simply tuned to different things.
en.editorNote: Send to the couple where one thinks the other's a nitpicker and the other thinks they're scatterbrained — nobody's broken, one's wired to see the tree and one the forest.
variations: Proofreading? Focused person. Big-picture planning? Peripheral person. Swap them and both lose their minds.
One sees the tree, one sees the forest. Human Design's Perspective setting explains why you and your partner never care about the same things.
